proposals.title: Evaluating the Clinical Impact and Alert Burden of Enhanced Surveillance for Multidrug-Resistant Gram-Negative Colonization in High-Risk Patients
proposals.motivation: Active surveillance for multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs) like ESBL-producing Enterobacterales is widely implemented to guide infection control and antibiotic therapy. However, it remains unclear whether the increased detection of colonization in high-risk patients (e.g., ICU, immunocompromised) leads to measurable improvements in patient outcomes, such as reduced CLABSI rates or mortality, or if it primarily generates alert fatigue and unnecessary antimicrobial exposure. This is a critical knowledge gap in antimicrobial stewardship and infection prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that enhanced MDRO surveillance in high-risk patients will not improve key clinical outcomes (e.g., time to appropriate therapy for subsequent infection, infection-related mortality) compared to standard clinical culture-based detection, and will be associated with a significant increase in antimicrobial alerts and interventions without clear benefit.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, retrospective cohort study at a tertiary care center, leveraging Dr. Cosgrove's prior work on ESBL colonization. High-risk patients (ICU, oncology) from 2020-2024 will be identified. The intervention group will consist of patients who underwent active rectal surveillance for ESBLs. The control group will be matched patients identified only via clinical cultures. Primary outcomes will be CLABSI incidence, infection-related mortality, and time to effective therapy for subsequent gram-negative infections. Secondary outcomes will quantify 'alert burden': the number of antimicrobial stewardship or infection control alerts generated per patient, and the rate of subsequent antibiotic changes (e.g., to cefepime or broader agents). Statistical analysis will use multivariate regression to adjust for confounders.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide evidence-based guidance on the real-world utility of active MDRO surveillance programs. It will quantify the potential trade-off between detection and alert fatigue, directly informing stewardship and infection control policy. The findings will help institutions optimize resource allocation by focusing surveillance on contexts where it demonstrably improves outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While surveillance for MDROs is common, this study moves beyond simple prevalence reporting to directly test the causal link between increased detection and patient-centered outcomes, integrating electronic health record data on clinical alerts and interventions—a methodologically novel approach building on Dr. Cosgrove's prior algorithm development work.

proposals.title: A Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of a Multi-Indicator Electronic Surveillance Algorithm for Early Detection of Healthcare-Associated Infections in High-Risk Patients
proposals.motivation: Healthcare-associated infections (HAIs), such as those caused by ESBL-producing organisms and CLABSI, impose a significant clinical and economic burden. Enhanced surveillance programs are resource-intensive, and their economic justification remains unclear. A rigorous, data-driven assessment of whether earlier detection via combined electronic indicators leads to net cost savings is needed to inform hospital infection prevention policy and resource allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: An electronic surveillance algorithm that integrates multiple clinical and microbiological indicators (e.g., antibiotic orders, culture results, lab values) will identify HAIs earlier than standard methods, and the resultant cost savings from averting prolonged illness and complications will outweigh the program implementation costs.
proposals.method: We will develop and validate a multi-indicator electronic algorithm using retrospective data from high-risk patient cohorts (e.g., ICU, oncology). The algorithm will synthesize data on antibiotic starts (e.g., cefepime, vancomycin), positive cultures for ESBL-producing Enterobacterales, renal function markers (creatinine), and device utilization. Using a decision-analytic model, we will compare the costs and outcomes (e.g., infection-related length-of-stay, mortality) of the enhanced surveillance pathway versus standard care, incorporating pre-2026 estimates for intervention costs, treatment costs, and quality-adjusted life years (QALYs).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, transparent cost-effectiveness model for electronic HAI surveillance, directly addressing a key gap in implementation science for infection prevention. It will offer hospital administrators and infection control committees an evidence-based framework to evaluate the return on investment for advanced surveillance systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Cosgrove and others has developed algorithms for specific outcomes (e.g., vancomycin-AKI, ESBL colonization), this proposal uniquely integrates these streams into a unified surveillance tool and explicitly evaluates its economic impact, moving beyond clinical validation to health economic justification.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a National-Scale Automated Surveillance System for Healthcare-Associated Infections Using a Multi-Indicator Algorithm
proposals.motivation: Healthcare-associated infections (HAIs), such as CLABSI and those caused by ESBL-producing organisms, remain a major source of patient morbidity and mortality, but surveillance is resource-intensive and inconsistent. Existing automated systems are siloed within single institutions, limiting the ability to track regional outbreaks, benchmark performance, and understand the national epidemiology of antimicrobial resistance. A scalable, standardized electronic surveillance system is urgently needed for public health.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-indicator algorithm, integrating microbiology, pharmacy, and clinical data from a nationwide EHR network, can accurately and efficiently identify HAIs at a population scale, enabling real-time surveillance and detection of emerging resistance patterns.
proposals.method: We will leverage a pre-existing, multi-institutional EHR data network (e.g., the National Patient-Centered Clinical Research Network - PCORnet, pre-2026). Building on Cosgrove's prior work on electronic AKI and pathogen exposure algorithms, we will develop a rules-based algorithm combining indicators: new positive cultures, specific antibiotic initiations (e.g., cefepime for Gram-negatives), fever, and leukocytosis. The system will use a cloud-based architecture for distributed querying and centralized analytics. Validation will involve manual chart review of flagged cases across participating sites to calculate sensitivity, specificity, and positive predictive value.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first validated, operational prototype for national HAI surveillance, moving beyond single-hospital pilots. It will provide a framework for real-time, standardized public health reporting and create a unique resource for studying the transmission dynamics of multidrug-resistant organisms like ESBL-producing Enterobacterales across the healthcare continuum.
proposals.novelty: While automated surveillance algorithms exist, scaling them to a heterogeneous, national EHR network with consistent performance is a major unsolved challenge. This proposal integrates a multi-indicator clinical logic with a federated data architecture specifically designed for population-level infection monitoring, a significant advancement over institution-specific tools.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Real-Time, Individualized Risk Score for Healthcare-Associated Infections in High-Risk Patients
proposals.motivation: Current infection surveillance operates at a population level, identifying outbreaks after they occur. For high-risk patients, such as those colonized with ESBL-producing Enterobacterales, this reactive approach fails to prevent individual infections. A shift to real-time, patient-specific risk prediction is needed to enable pre-emptive interventions and improve outcomes, particularly for infections like CLABSI.
proposals.hypothesis: A machine learning model integrating dynamic, patient-specific data from the electronic health record (EHR) and prior colonization status (e.g., ESBL) can generate a real-time, validated risk score predictive of imminent healthcare-associated infection.
proposals.method: We will develop a retrospective cohort using EHR data from high-risk patients (e.g., ICU, oncology). Predictors will include real-time vital signs, laboratory values (e.g., creatinine for kidney injury risk), device utilization, antibiotic exposure (e.g., cefepime, vancomycin), and prior microbiological data (e.g., ESBL colonization isolates). We will train a gradient boosting model (XGBoost) to output a time-varying infection probability. Model performance will be validated against confirmed infection events.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a clinically actionable, real-time risk score that moves infection prevention from surveillance to prediction at the bedside. It will provide a framework for integrating complex, time-sensitive data streams, including colonization status, to target preventive resources effectively.
proposals.novelty: While population-level risk models exist, this approach dynamically scales prediction to the individual patient level by leveraging continuous EHR data streams and prior colonization history, a refinement beyond static risk factors.

proposals.title: Adaptation of an Electronic Algorithm for Vancomycin-Associated Acute Kidney Injury Surveillance in Low-Resource Hospitals with Sparse Electronic Data
proposals.motivation: Electronic surveillance algorithms for adverse drug events, like vancomycin-associated acute kidney injury (VA-AKI), are validated in high-resource settings with comprehensive electronic health records (EHRs). However, hospitals in low-resource settings often lack integrated pharmacy, laboratory, and coding systems, creating a surveillance gap. This leads to under-detection of iatrogenic harm and missed opportunities for antimicrobial stewardship and patient safety interventions in these high-burden environments.
proposals.hypothesis: A simplified, rule-based electronic algorithm, using a minimal set of routinely available laboratory data (e.g., serum creatinine), can be deployed as a standalone tool to identify probable VA-AKI cases with acceptable sensitivity and specificity in hospitals lacking comprehensive EHRs.
proposals.method: We will adapt the previously published VA-AKI algorithm by deconstructing its logic and identifying core components feasible with limited data. The primary adaptation will replace pharmacy administration timestamps with a patient-day-level exposure flag based on known vancomycin orders or ward-level administration logs. The algorithm will use serial serum creatinine values, applying KDIGO criteria, sourced from either a basic laboratory information system or manual data entry forms. We will implement this as a lightweight, offline-capable dashboard (e.g., using R Shiny or a simple Python script) for periodic batch processing of lab exports, generating case alerts for clinician review.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, pragmatic tool for VA-AKI surveillance in low-resource hospitals, enabling baseline measurement of a key antimicrobial safety metric. It will demonstrate a methodology for 'de-scaling' complex electronic algorithms to lower-data environments, contributing to global health equity in patient safety research and antimicrobial stewardship.
proposals.novelty: While algorithm adaptation for resource limitations is common in some fields, applying this specifically to pharmacovigilance and antimicrobial safety surveillance in low-resource hospitals is novel. It moves beyond colonization studies (like ESBL work) to active harm detection, leveraging Cosgrove's expertise in electronic algorithm development for new implementation contexts.

proposals.title: A 'Planetary Protection' Paradigm for Stewardship: A Randomized Trial of Metagenomic Sequencing-Guided Antibiotic Initiation in Severe Viral Pneumonia
proposals.motivation: Empiric antibiotic use in COVID-19 and influenza pneumonia is widespread, driven by concerns for bacterial co-infection, yet pre-2025 studies suggest confirmed co-infection rates are low (often <10%). This practice contributes to antimicrobial resistance, adverse drug events like nephrotoxicity, and Clostridioides difficile infection. A paradigm shift from empiric to evidence-based antibiotic initiation is urgently needed to improve patient outcomes and preserve antibiotic efficacy.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients hospitalized with severe viral pneumonia, a management protocol mandating rapid lower respiratory tract metagenomic sequencing prior to antibiotic initiation will result in significantly reduced antibiotic exposure without increasing mortality or treatment failure, compared to standard empiric therapy guided by conventional cultures.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, randomized controlled trial in ICU patients with confirmed severe viral (COVID-19/influenza) pneumonia. The intervention arm will undergo rapid (<8-hour) metagenomic next-generation sequencing (mNGS) of bronchoalveolar lavage or tracheal aspirate samples using established platforms (e.g., IDbyDNA). Antibiotics will be initiated or withheld based on a pre-specified algorithm triggered by the detection of specific bacterial pathogens above a validated threshold. The control arm will receive standard care per institutional guidelines. The primary outcome is antibiotic days of therapy in the first 7 days. Secondary outcomes include 30-day mortality, clinical failure, and rates of hospital-onset CDI and MDRO colonization, leveraging Dr. Cosgrove's expertise in ESBL surveillance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-level evidence for a diagnostic-driven, 'zero-empiric' antibiotic strategy in viral pneumonia. It will generate a replicable clinical algorithm for mNGS-guided stewardship and quantify the impact on patient safety (e.g., reduced AKI from vancomycin) and ecological pressure favoring resistant organisms like ESBL-producing Enterobacterales.
proposals.novelty: The conceptual innovation lies in applying the 'planetary protection' logic of 'assay before action' and 'targeted containment' to clinical microbiology, fundamentally challenging the risk-aversion underpinning empiricism. Methodologically, it moves beyond using mNGS as a diagnostic tool of last resort to positioning it as a primary, rule-out test for antibiotic necessity.

proposals.title: Calibrating Consensus Co-infection Definitions for SARS-CoV-2 Variants and Diverse Patient Populations: A Phylogenetic Validation Approach
proposals.motivation: Consensus definitions for SARS-CoV-2 co-infections are critical for clinical management and research but were developed primarily during the early pandemic. As the virus evolves into new variants (e.g., Omicron sub-lineages) and is applied to diverse patient populations, the performance of these definitions—often reliant on syndromic criteria and PCR cycle threshold (Ct) values—is unknown. Uncalibrated definitions risk misclassifying co-infections, leading to inappropriate antibiotic use and flawed epidemiological data.
proposals.hypothesis: The performance of consensus co-infection definitions degrades when applied to later SARS-CoV-2 variants and distinct patient populations, but this performance can be quantitatively assessed and calibrated using a phylogenetic model validation framework to maintain diagnostic accuracy.
proposals.method: We will apply existing co-infection definitions as a baseline model to retrospective and prospective cohorts infected with later variants (post-Delta) across diverse geographic/patient groups. Using linked microbiological data (e.g., multiplex PCR, culture of respiratory isolates) and clinical outcomes as a gold standard, we will quantify definition performance (sensitivity, specificity). We will adapt phylogenetic calibration methods, treating the definition as a 'molecular clock' model, to test its 'fit' using likelihood-ratio tests and identify parameters requiring adjustment (e.g., optimal Ct value cut-offs, syndromic criteria weights) for each variant/population context.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first evidence-based framework for validating and calibrating co-infection definitions across the evolving pandemic, directly supporting consistent clinical diagnosis and antimicrobial stewardship. It will yield variant- and population-specific calibration parameters that can be integrated into electronic clinical decision support algorithms, like those Dr. Cosgrove has previously developed.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in adapting phylogenetic model calibration—a core method in evolutionary biology—to the problem of diagnostic definition validation, a non-obvious cross-disciplinary analogy. It moves beyond simply applying definitions to actively testing and adjusting their parameters for new contexts.

proposals.title: A Dynamic Predictive Allocation Model for Empiric Antibiotic Therapy in COVID-19 Admissions: A Stewardship Intervention Study
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic has complicated antimicrobial stewardship, with high rates of empiric antibiotic use for suspected bacterial co-infection, driving resistance and C. difficile risk. Current stewardship is often reactive, lacking a framework to dynamically allocate limited-spectrum agents to the highest-risk patients while conserving broader agents, analogous to managing a constrained supply chain during volatile demand.
proposals.hypothesis: A clinical decision support (CDS) tool using a predictive resource allocation algorithm, adapted from supply chain logistics, will optimize empiric antibiotic selection in COVID-19 admissions, reducing unnecessary use of broad-spectrum agents like cefepime without compromising patient outcomes.
proposals.method: We will develop a model using historical data (patient vitals, lab results, local ESBL colonization rates, prior antibiotic use) to forecast individual patient risk for resistant infection. This model will employ a reinforcement learning architecture, treating antibiotic classes as inventory and patient risk as demand, to recommend an optimal empiric therapy. The algorithm will be integrated into an electronic health record CDS for a pilot ward, with outcomes compared to a control period using interrupted time-series analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, operational framework for proactive antimicrobial stewardship that dynamically balances individual patient risk with population-level antibiotic preservation. It will yield a scalable CDS tool and evidence on whether logistics-inspired predictive allocation can safely reduce broad-spectrum antibiotic use in high-acuity settings.
proposals.novelty: While predictive models for infection risk exist, this approach uniquely frames stewardship as a real-time resource allocation problem, applying supply chain optimization algorithms not previously used for antibiotic decision-making at the point of care.

proposals.title: A Global Multicenter Surveillance Network for Co-Infections and Antimicrobial Resistance: Validating a Consensus Definition Across Diverse Healthcare Settings
proposals.motivation: The rise of multi-drug resistant organisms (MDROs) like ESBL-producing Enterobacterales and the complexity of co-infections present a critical, global public health threat. Existing surveillance is often siloed within single healthcare systems or regions, limiting our understanding of the true epidemiology, risk factors, and outcomes of co-infections in varied resource and infection control contexts. A harmonized, scalable surveillance approach is urgently needed to inform global antibiotic stewardship and infection prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: A consensus electronic definition for bloodstream co-infections, developed and validated in a five-hospital network, can be successfully implemented and yield comparable, high-fidelity epidemiological data across a global network of hospitals with diverse patient populations, laboratory practices, and electronic health record systems.
proposals.method: We will leverage the existing electronic algorithm framework from Dr. Cosgrove's prior work on vancomycin-associated AKI and pathogen exposure. The method involves adapting the consensus co-infection definition into a modular, interoperable algorithm using standardized data elements (e.g., microbiology results, timing, antibiotic administration). This will be deployed via a secure, cloud-based platform (akin to the HIPPER system concept) to participating global sites. Sites will run the algorithm on local isolates, including ESBL producers, with centralized validation of case adjudication and periodic data harmonization rounds to ensure consistency.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first operational framework for global, real-time surveillance of bloodstream co-infections, directly addressing a WHO priority. It will generate the largest, most geographically diverse dataset on co-infection epidemiology, enabling analyses of regional variations in pathogen prevalence, resistance patterns (e.g., to cefepime), and outcomes. The network itself will serve as a foundational platform for future interventional studies and rapid response to emerging threats.
proposals.novelty: While multicenter studies exist, this proposal moves beyond regional collaboration to create a scalable, *electronic algorithm-driven* global surveillance network specifically for co-infections, integrating the case definition validation process directly into diverse hospital data infrastructures rather than relying solely on manual chart review.

proposals.title: Predictors of Sustained Central Line-Associated Bloodstream Infection Reduction in the ICU: A Multi-Center, Mixed-Methods Analysis of Organizational and Cultural Factors
proposals.motivation: Despite initial success, many ICU infection prevention initiatives experience performance decay over time, leading to preventable harm and wasted resources. Understanding the determinants of sustained improvement is critical for healthcare systems. This study addresses a key gap by moving beyond implementation to identify factors that predict long-term sustainability of CLABSI reduction at the individual ICU level.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sustained CLABSI reduction in ICUs is predicted by a core set of measurable organizational factors (e.g., leadership stability, audit-and-feedback frequency) and cultural factors (e.g., psychological safety, shared accountability) that are distinct from factors associated only with initial implementation success.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of ICUs with established CLABSI prevention programs, using a convergent parallel mixed-methods design. Quantitative analysis will use electronic health record and administrative data to model CLABSI rate trajectories, defining 'sustained' vs. 'decaying' cohorts. We will then administer validated surveys (e.g., Safety Attitudes Questionnaire) and conduct structured interviews with key personnel (clinicians, infection preventionists, unit leadership) in a purposively sampled subset of ICUs. Predictors will be identified using multivariate regression and thematic analysis, integrated to build a predictive framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, actionable set of predictors for sustainability, enabling healthcare systems to proactively identify ICUs at risk for performance decay and target supportive interventions. It will advance the science of implementation by shifting focus from adoption to maintenance, directly informing quality improvement strategy and resource allocation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies barriers to implementation, this study uniquely applies longitudinal, unit-level outcome data to empirically link specific organizational and cultural constructs with sustained infection reduction, moving beyond qualitative description to predictive modeling.

proposals.title: A Scalable Electronic Intervention for Prevention of Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia and Catheter-Associated Urinary Tract Infections: A Quasi-Experimental Study
proposals.motivation: Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) and catheter-associated urinary tract infections (CAUTI) remain leading causes of healthcare-associated infections (HAIs), morbidity, and excess cost, despite established prevention bundles. Translating bundle compliance into sustained practice is challenging. The success of electronic checklist-plus-feedback systems for central line-associated bloodstream infection (CLABSI) prevention, as evidenced by Dr. Cosgrove's prior work, suggests a scalable model for other device-associated infections.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing an electronic, algorithm-driven checklist with real-time feedback for clinicians, modeled on the successful CLABSI intervention, will significantly increase adherence to VAP and CAUTI prevention bundles and reduce the incidence rates of these infections in high-risk patient populations.
proposals.method: We will adapt the existing CLABSI electronic framework (e.g., from the 'Healthcare personnel interactive pathogen exposure response system') to incorporate VAP and CAUTI prevention elements from CDC/NHSN guidelines. The system will use rule-based algorithms to identify eligible patients (e.g., mechanically ventilated, with indwelling urinary catheters), prompt bedside nurses for daily bundle compliance via the EHR, and provide unit-level audit feedback to nurse managers. A quasi-experimental, interrupted time-series analysis will compare bundle adherence and infection rates (per 1000 device-days) pre- and post-implementation across multiple ICUs, using negative binomial regression models adjusted for confounders like patient acuity and antimicrobial use (e.g., cefepime, ESBL-producing organism colonization status).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide robust evidence for the effectiveness of a unified electronic platform in preventing multiple device-associated HAIs, moving beyond single-infection silos. It will yield a generalizable implementation blueprint and a validated tool for sustaining compliance with complex prevention protocols, directly contributing to patient safety and antimicrobial stewardship goals.
proposals.novelty: While checklists for VAP/CAUTI exist, this proposal integrates them into a single, proactive, feedback-driven system already proven for CLABSI, creating a holistic 'device-associated infection' prevention dashboard. The novel linkage of compliance data with patient-level microbiological data (e.g., ESBL colonization) will allow for stratified analysis of intervention impact in highest-risk cohorts.

proposals.title: Time-to-Relapse Analysis of Central Line-Associated Bloodstream Infection (CLABSI) Rates in Intensive Care Units: A Multi-Center Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Infection prevention bundles have reduced CLABSI rates, but sustaining these gains is challenging. Rebound events threaten patient safety and represent a failure of continuous quality improvement. Pre-2026, most analyses focus on point prevalence or aggregate trends, lacking a formal framework to model the timing and predictors of when individual ICUs first experience a significant rate increase, or 'relapse', after a period of control.
proposals.hypothesis: The time to first CLABSI rate relapse in an ICU follows a predictable pattern and is associated with modifiable unit-level factors, including staffing ratios, antimicrobial stewardship metrics (e.g., cefepime and ESBL-producer prevalence), and patient colonization pressure with multi-drug resistant organisms.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, multi-center cohort study of adult ICUs, defining 'relapse' as a statistically significant increase in CLABSI rates over a 3-month moving window compared to a 12-month baseline control period. Using data from electronic health records and the National Healthcare Safety Network, we will apply survival analysis methods (Kaplan-Meier estimation and Cox proportional hazards regression). The model will treat each ICU as a subject, with time zero defined as the start of the control period. Censoring will occur at the end of follow-up or unit closure. Key covariates will include unit-level antibiotic use (DDD/1000 patient-days), prevalence of ESBL-producing Enterobacterales from rectal surveillance isolates, nurse-to-patient ratios, and consultant/advisory team engagement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, time-to-event metric for infection prevention program resilience. It will identify high-risk periods and modifiable risk factors for CLABSI rate rebound, enabling targeted, pre-emptive interventions. The methodology will offer a new analytical framework for evaluating the durability of other healthcare-associated infection prevention efforts.
proposals.novelty: While survival analysis is standard in clinical trials, its application to model the 'failure' of an infection prevention program at the unit level is novel. This approach moves beyond descriptive epidemiology to formally model the risk of recidivism, integrating clinical microbiology data (ESBL isolates) and healthcare operations factors.

proposals.title: A Ward-Level Implementation and Evaluation of a Central Line-Associated Bloodstream Infection (CLABSI) Prevention Bundle in High-Risk Oncology and Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplant Units
proposals.motivation: ICU-level collaboratives have successfully reduced CLABSI rates, but resource-intensive, unit-wide interventions are difficult to implement in specialized wards like oncology and outpatient infusion centers, where patients with prolonged neutropenia and central lines are at extreme risk. Pre-2026, there is a gap in scalable, data-driven models for these high-risk, non-ICU settings, which often harbor extended-spectrum β-lactamase (ESBL)-producing organisms, making infections more lethal.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting and implementing a targeted, electronic health record (EHR)-enabled CLABSI prevention bundle at the individual ward level will significantly reduce CLABSI rates and associated gram-negative bacteremia in high-risk hematology-oncology units compared to pre-intervention periods and matched control wards.
proposals.method: We will conduct a stepped-wedge, cluster-randomized trial across 8-10 hospital wards. The intervention adapts the ICU collaborative model to a ward-level 'Healthcare Personnel Interactive Pathogen Exposure Response' system, using an electronic algorithm (akin to the vancomycin-AKI tool) to flag high-risk patients (e.g., ESBL colonization, prolonged central access). The bundle includes ward-specific checklists, audit/feedback via a dashboard, and real-time alerts for line necessity and dressing integrity. Primary outcome is CLABSI rate per 1000 central-line days; secondary outcomes include cefepime-resistant gram-negative bacteremia and bundle adherence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, scalable framework for implementing precision infection prevention in resource-constrained, high-risk ward settings. It will generate evidence on the effectiveness of ward-level collaboratives and produce an adaptable EHR-based tool for real-time risk stratification and intervention, directly addressing the challenge of antimicrobial resistance in vulnerable populations.
proposals.novelty: While ICU models exist, this systematically adapts the collaborative approach for non-ICU wards with distinct patient populations and workflows, and integrates real-time, patient-specific ESBL colonization data (from Cosgrove's prior work) to target interventions, moving beyond one-size-fits-all bundles.

proposals.title: Adaptation and Feasibility of a Simplified, Resource-Appropriate CLABSI Prevention Bundle for ICUs in Low-Resource International Hospital Settings
proposals.motivation: Central line-associated bloodstream infections (CLABSIs) are a major cause of mortality and morbidity in ICUs globally, with a disproportionate burden in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs). While five-component checklist interventions have proven effective in high-resource settings, their direct implementation is often hindered in LMICs by resource constraints, including limited access to antimicrobial catheters, advanced electronic surveillance, and dedicated infection prevention staff. Adapting evidence-based interventions to these settings is a critical, unmet need in global health.
proposals.hypothesis: A contextually adapted, simplified CLABSI prevention bundle, derived from the five-component checklist but modified for resource limitations, will be feasible to implement and will lead to a significant reduction in CLABSI rates in ICUs of partner LMIC hospitals.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-phase study in partnership with 2-3 hospitals in LMICs. First, a needs assessment and barrier analysis will inform the adaptation of the checklist, prioritizing low-cost, high-impact components (e.g., enhanced hand hygiene, chlorhexidine skin antisepsis, audit-and-feedback). We will then implement the adapted bundle using a stepped-wedge cluster design. Data collection will leverage the researcher's experience with electronic algorithms (e.g., for AKI) but will be simplified for low-tech settings, using structured paper forms and periodic audits. CLABSI rates will be tracked using standardized CDC/NHSN definitions adapted to local microbiology capacity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, context-specific CLABSI prevention protocol for resource-limited ICUs, directly addressing a key gap in global infection control. It will provide practical evidence on implementing antimicrobial stewardship and infection prevention interventions in settings with high rates of multidrug-resistant organisms (e.g., ESBL-producing Enterobacterales), contributing to the reduction of healthcare-associated infections and associated antibiotic use.
proposals.novelty: While checklist bundles are well-studied, this proposal specifically focuses on systematic adaptation and de-implementation of resource-intensive components for LMIC ICUs, moving beyond simple translation. It integrates the researcher's expertise in electronic clinical decision support with pragmatic, low-tech implementation science in international partner hospitals.

proposals.title: Quantifying Antibiotic Exposure Heterogeneity: A Multi-Dimensional Algorithm to Refine Risk Models for ESBL Colonization and Cefepime Resistance
proposals.motivation: Current electronic health record (EHR)-based studies of antibiotic exposure and outcomes, such as colonization with ESBL-producing Enterobacterales or the development of resistance, often treat an antibiotic order as a binary exposure. This crude measure ignores critical pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic dimensions like dose, duration, and route, which directly impact selective pressure on the gut microbiome. This methodological gap limits the precision of risk prediction and hinders the development of targeted stewardship interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-dimensional antibiotic exposure score, incorporating dose, duration, and route, will be a stronger predictor of subsequent gastrointestinal colonization by ESBL-producing organisms and the emergence of cefepime non-susceptibility in clinical isolates than a binary (exposed/unexposed) model.
proposals.method: We will develop and validate an electronic algorithm that extracts and weights antibiotic exposure data from the EHR. For each antibiotic administered (focusing on beta-lactams, including cefepime, and fluoroquinolones), the algorithm will calculate a time-weighted exposure score integrating daily dose (normalized by defined daily dose), administration route (IV vs. oral), and duration. This score will be linked to two pre-2026 outcomes: 1) new ESBL colonization from active surveillance cultures in high-risk patients (leveraging prior work), and 2) isolation of cefepime-non-susceptible organisms from clinical cultures. Multivariable regression will compare the predictive power of the continuous exposure score against traditional binary measures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, scalable tool for quantifying real-world antibiotic pressure, moving the field beyond binary exposure definitions. It will deliver more accurate risk estimates for key antimicrobial resistance outcomes, directly informing stewardship programs. The algorithm can be integrated into clinical decision support or exposure response systems, like HIPER, for real-time risk assessment.
proposals.novelty: While algorithms exist to identify drug-related adverse events (e.g., vancomycin-AKI), applying a similar multi-parameter computational approach to model antibiotic exposure as a continuous driver of microbial resistance in the gut is novel. It directly challenges the standard epidemiological assumption of uniform exposure.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Machine Learning Model for Predicting Post-Discharge Surgical Site Infections in High-Risk Surgical Cohorts
proposals.motivation: A significant proportion of surgical site infections (SSIs) are diagnosed after hospital discharge, leading to readmissions, increased morbidity, and healthcare costs. Traditional surveillance often misses these post-discharge events, creating a critical gap in quality measurement and patient safety. This is especially pertinent for patients colonized with multi-drug resistant organisms (MDROs), like ESBL-producing Enterobacterales, who are at heightened risk for complicated infections.
proposals.hypothesis: A predictive model incorporating pre-operative patient factors (including MDRO colonization status), procedural details, and perioperative data can accurately stratify the risk of post-discharge SSI, outperforming traditional, procedure-only risk indices.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of patients undergoing eligible surgeries at a tertiary care center, leveraging electronic health record data and existing surveillance infrastructure (e.g., HIPPER). Candidate predictors will include demographics, comorbidities, pre-operative MDRO colonization (extending Cosgrove's work on ESBL colonization), procedure type/duration, and perioperative antibiotic use. We will train and compare multiple machine learning algorithms, including logistic regression with regularization (LASSO) and gradient boosting machines (XGBoost), using a temporal validation split. Model performance will be assessed via AUC, calibration plots, and decision curve analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, clinically actionable tool for identifying patients at highest risk for post-discharge SSI, enabling targeted post-discharge monitoring and intervention. It will directly extend infection prevention efforts beyond the hospital walls and provide novel insights into the role of MDRO colonization as a modifiable risk factor for SSI.
proposals.novelty: While prior SSI models focus on in-hospital detection, this proposal uniquely integrates pre-operative MDRO colonization data—a specific, high-risk exposure—with comprehensive procedural data to predict outcomes in the largely unmonitored post-discharge period.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Structured Telemedicine Wound Monitoring Program to Reduce Post-Discharge Surgical Site Infection Morbidity
proposals.motivation: A significant proportion of surgical site infections (SSIs) are diagnosed after hospital discharge, leading to delayed treatment, increased readmissions, and worse patient outcomes. Pre-2026 surveillance is largely hospital-based, creating a critical gap in detection and management. This is especially relevant for high-risk patients, such as those colonized with multi-drug resistant organisms like ESBL-producing Enterobacterales, who face greater complications from post-discharge SSIs.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a structured, algorithm-driven telemedicine wound monitoring program for high-risk surgical patients post-discharge will increase SSI detection rates, reduce time to appropriate antibiotic therapy, and decrease 30-day readmission rates compared to standard care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-arm, patient-level randomized controlled trial. High-risk patients (e.g., colorectal surgery, history of MDRO colonization) will be randomized to intervention or standard care. The intervention arm will use a secure mobile platform for daily patient-submitted wound photos and symptom surveys for 30 days post-discharge. An electronic algorithm, building on Dr. Cosgrove's prior AKI work, will flag concerning features (e.g., erythema, drainage) for immediate review by an infection prevention nurse or surgeon. Isolates from confirmed SSIs will undergo microbiological characterization, including ESBL testing and susceptibility to agents like cefepime.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence for the effectiveness of a scalable post-discharge SSI surveillance intervention. It will generate novel data on the microbiology and outcomes of SSIs detected in the community setting, particularly among MDRO-colonized patients. Findings will directly inform best practices for health system transitions of care and antimicrobial stewardship.
proposals.novelty: While telemedicine exists, the novelty lies in the targeted application to post-discharge SSI surveillance for a high-risk cohort, integrated with a validated electronic alert algorithm for proactive clinical response, and paired with detailed microbiological analysis to link intervention to pathogen-specific outcomes.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Post-Discharge Surgical Site Infection Detection Algorithm Using National Insurance Claims Data
proposals.motivation: Surgical site infections (SSIs) are a major cause of post-discharge morbidity, readmission, and cost, but traditional surveillance is resource-intensive and often misses post-discharge events. Leveraging existing electronic data for automated detection could dramatically improve surveillance efficiency and outbreak response. Pre-2026, pharmacy claims algorithms have proven effective for chronic disease epidemiology but remain under-explored for acute, post-procedure infectious outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: A machine learning algorithm trained on patterns of antibiotic prescriptions, outpatient encounters, and diagnostic codes in insurance claims data can accurately identify post-discharge SSIs with performance comparable to traditional manual chart review.
proposals.method: We will adapt methods from chronic disease detection (e.g., for diabetes) to the acute SSI context. Using a national private claims database (e.g., IBM MarketScan), we will identify patients undergoing major surgeries. The algorithm will integrate temporal sequences of post-discharge claims for specific antibiotics (e.g., cefepime, agents for ESBL), wound care, emergency department visits, and re-admissions. We will validate the algorithm against a gold-standard cohort with confirmed SSIs via manual chart review, similar to Dr. Cosgrove's prior work validating vancomycin-AKI algorithms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, automated tool for health systems and payers to monitor post-discharge SSI rates across populations, enabling targeted quality improvement. It will establish a novel application of claims-based epidemiology to surgical outcomes, creating a model transferable to other healthcare-associated infections like post-discharge CLABSI.
proposals.novelty: While claims algorithms exist for chronic conditions, applying this methodology to detect acute, procedure-specific infections like SSI is novel. The approach uniquely focuses on the post-discharge period, a critical surveillance gap, by leveraging the temporal sequence of pharmacy and encounter data.

proposals.title: Comparative Analysis of Methicillin-Susceptible Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA) Epidemiology and Outcomes in Tertiary vs. Community Hospital Settings: A Multi-Center Validation Study
proposals.motivation: Research on antimicrobial resistance and infection outcomes is often conducted at large academic centers, where patient acuity, antimicrobial exposure, and resistance patterns are distinct. This creates a critical evidence gap for community hospitals, which manage the majority of patients. Generalizing findings on pathogens like MSSA from tertiary centers can lead to suboptimal empiric therapy and stewardship policies in community settings, potentially impacting patient outcomes and resource use.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the clinical characteristics, antimicrobial susceptibility profiles, and incidence of complications (e.g., bacteremia, acute kidney injury) for MSSA infections differ significantly between tertiary-care and community hospital populations, rendering single-center, tertiary-care-derived algorithms for management less applicable broadly.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, multi-center cohort study using electronic health record data from one tertiary-care academic center and three community hospitals within the same healthcare system (pre-2026 data). The study will include adult inpatients with culture-confirmed MSSA infections. We will adapt and apply the electronic algorithm methodology from Cosgrove's prior work on vancomycin-associated AKI to systematically identify and compare outcomes. Variables will include demographics, comorbidities, source of infection, prior antibiotic exposure (e.g., cefepime), susceptibility patterns, and development of complications. Statistical analysis will involve multivariable regression to identify setting-specific risk factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly quantify the generalizability gap for MSSA management evidence. It will provide community hospitals with setting-specific epidemiological data to inform local antimicrobial stewardship and empirical treatment guidelines. The study will also demonstrate a framework for validating single-center algorithms across diverse care settings, a model applicable to other pathogens like ESBL-producing Enterobacterales.
proposals.novelty: While the limitation of single-center studies is acknowledged, there is a paucity of empirical, multi-center work directly comparing the epidemiology of common pathogens like MSSA across hospital types to validate this concern. This study leverages established electronic phenotyping algorithms in a novel, comparative context to test generalizability explicitly.

proposals.title: A Retrospective Cohort Study to Define the Optimal Window for De-escalation from Empiric Vancomycin to Beta-Lactam Therapy in Patients with Methicillin-Susceptible Staphylococcus aureus Bacteremia
proposals.motivation: Empiric vancomycin is commonly used for suspected staphylococcal infections, but its prolonged use after identification of methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA) is associated with increased nephrotoxicity and suboptimal outcomes compared to definitive beta-lactam therapy. Current guidelines recommend de-escalation but lack evidence-based, time-specific targets for the switch, leading to practice variation and potential patient harm. This directly addresses Dr. Cosgrove's prior work on vancomycin-associated acute kidney injury (VA-AKI) and antimicrobial stewardship in high-risk patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that there exists a critical time window (e.g., <24-48 hours) from the reporting of MSSA susceptibility results to the discontinuation of vancomycin, beyond which the risk of VA-AKI increases significantly without improving clinical cure rates.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center retrospective cohort study of adult inpatients with MSSA bacteremia who received empiric vancomycin. Using electronic health record data, we will apply and extend our previously validated electronic algorithm for VA-AKI to precisely time kidney injury events. The primary exposure will be the time from the first positive MSSA culture result to vancomycin discontinuation, categorized into intervals. We will use multivariable logistic regression, adjusting for APACHE-II scores, concomitant nephrotoxins, and source of bacteremia, to model the independent association between switch timing and the composite outcome of VA-AKI or treatment failure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative evidence to define an optimal, actionable de-escalation window for MSSA bacteremia, moving stewardship from a recommendation to a time-sensitive intervention. It will directly inform clinical decision support tools and antimicrobial policy, aiming to reduce VA-AKI rates and improve patient safety. The findings will be integrated into stewardship pathways similar to the 'interactive pathogen exposure response system' concept for other multidrug-resistant organisms.
proposals.novelty: While the need for de-escalation is known, this study uniquely applies precise, algorithm-driven AKI phenotyping and time-to-event analysis to define a specific, evidence-based deadline for therapy switch, an approach not yet established in the literature. It synergistically combines Dr. Cosgrove's expertise in VA-AKI detection and high-risk patient colonization to solve a targeted stewardship problem.

proposals.title: Target Trial Emulation of Cefepime to Carbapenem Switching in ESBL-Producing Enterobacterales Bacteremia: A Causal Inference Analysis of Clinical Outcomes
proposals.motivation: For patients with bloodstream infections from ESBL-producing Enterobacterales, the optimal timing for switching from empirical cefepime to a carbapenem remains unclear. Observational studies on switching strategies are plagued by confounding by indication, where sicker patients are more likely to be switched, biasing effect estimates. A rigorous causal framework is needed to inform this high-stakes clinical decision and potentially reduce carbapenem overuse.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, after emulating a target trial to minimize confounding, a protocol-defined switch from cefepime to a carbapenem upon ESBL confirmation will be associated with reduced 30-day mortality compared to continuing cefepime, but that this benefit will be concentrated in patients with high-risk source or severity markers.
proposals.method: We will apply the target trial emulation framework to retrospective cohorts from prior studies (e.g., Cosgrove et al., 2487). Using electronic health record data, we will explicitly define eligibility, treatment strategies (switch vs. continue), assignment procedures, outcomes (mortality, recurrent bacteremia), and follow-up. Causal contrasts will be estimated using inverse probability of treatment weighting (IPTW) to adjust for time-fixed and time-varying confounders (e.g., sequential organ failure assessment scores, source control). Subgroup analyses will focus on patients with gastrointestinal colonization vs. other sources.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a model for applying causal inference methods to antibiotic stewardship questions, moving beyond associative analyses. It will generate actionable evidence for a defined switching rule in ESBL bacteremia, potentially improving outcomes and preserving carbapenems. The emulation protocol can be adapted for other antibiotic comparative effectiveness studies.
proposals.novelty: While target trial emulation is emerging in cardiology and oncology, its application to antimicrobial stewardship, particularly the dynamic decision of antibiotic switching, is novel. This study integrates Dr. Cosgrove's expertise in ESBL epidemiology and electronic algorithm development to address a core causal question in infectious diseases.

proposals.title: Multi-Center Validation of an Electronic Algorithm for Predicting Cefepime-Associated Acute Kidney Injury in Patients with ESBL-Producing Enterobacterales Bacteremia
proposals.motivation: Cefepime is a first-line therapy for serious infections caused by ESBL-producing Enterobacterales (ESBL-E), but it carries a known risk of acute kidney injury (AKI). Pre-2026, Dr. Cosgrove's team developed an electronic algorithm for vancomycin-associated AKI, demonstrating the utility of automated surveillance. Validating a similar tool for cefepime in a high-risk, ESBL-E population across diverse hospital settings is critical for patient safety and antimicrobial stewardship, as current risk assessment is inconsistent.
proposals.hypothesis: An electronic health record-based algorithm, adapted from the vancomycin-AKI model, can accurately identify cefepime-associated AKI in patients with ESBL-E bacteremia, and its performance will be generalizable across community, academic, and safety-net hospital systems.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center retrospective cohort study using data from 2018-2025. The algorithm will be adapted from the previously validated vancomycin-AKI model, incorporating cefepime dosing, duration, trough levels (where available), and established nephrotoxicity risk factors. The primary outcome will be AKI defined by KDIGO criteria. We will apply the algorithm to data from at least three hospitals representing community, academic, and safety-net systems. Performance will be assessed via sensitivity, specificity, and positive predictive value, with manual chart review as the gold standard for a subset of cases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first multi-center validated tool for automated surveillance of cefepime-associated AKI in a high-risk ESBL-E population. It will directly inform antimicrobial stewardship and supportive care protocols. The findings will establish the generalizability of electronic AKI algorithms across diverse healthcare delivery models, a crucial step for widespread implementation.
proposals.novelty: While electronic AKI algorithms exist for vancomycin, applying and validating this methodology specifically for cefepime in the context of ESBL-E infections across heterogeneous hospital systems is novel. It bridges Dr. Cosgrove's prior work on AKI algorithms, pathogen exposure systems, and ESBL-E colonization into a unified, scalable clinical prediction tool.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of Cefazolin vs. Anti-Staphylococcal Penicillins in Methicillin-Susceptible Staphylococcus aureus Infective Endocarditis: A Retrospective Cohort Study Using an Electronic Phenotyping Algorithm
proposals.motivation: Infective endocarditis (IE) due to MSSA carries high mortality and morbidity. The choice between cefazolin (CFZ) and anti-staphylococcal penicillins (ASPs) like nafcillin remains controversial, with observational data before 2026 showing conflicting results on treatment failure and nephrotoxicity. This uncertainty is most critical in IE, where antibiotic efficacy directly impacts valve destruction, metastatic infection, and survival, yet prior studies often aggregate heterogeneous infection types.
proposals.hypothesis: Among patients with MSSA IE, treatment with CFZ is non-inferior to ASPs in terms of a composite outcome of treatment failure (including mortality, relapse, or need for valve surgery) and is associated with a lower incidence of acute kidney injury (AKI).
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center retrospective cohort study of adult patients with MSSA IE from 2015-2025. IE will be identified using a validated electronic health record algorithm based on modified Duke criteria, integrating blood culture data, echocardiography reports, and clinical notes via natural language processing. The primary exposure is definitive therapy with CFZ or an ASP. The primary outcome is a composite of 90-day all-cause mortality, IE relapse, or valvular surgery. AKI will be identified using a previously validated electronic algorithm (akin to Cosgrove's vancomycin-AKI work). We will use propensity score matching to adjust for confounders and perform time-to-event analyses.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-quality, infection-specific evidence to guide antibiotic selection in a high-stakes condition, potentially supporting the safer use of CFZ in MSSA IE. It will demonstrate the application of electronic phenotyping algorithms for precise cohort identification in antimicrobial stewardship research, a method directly relevant to the researcher's prior work on ESBL colonization and algorithmic AKI detection.
proposals.novelty: This proposal moves beyond aggregated bacteremia studies to focus exclusively on IE, the patient subgroup where antibiotic choice is prognostically most significant. It leverages the researcher's expertise in electronic algorithm development for precise patient phenotyping (IE) and outcome ascertainment (AKI) to address a long-standing therapeutic dilemma.

proposals.title: Feasibility and Outcomes of Oral Step-Down Therapy for Gram-Negative Bacteremia in High-Risk Immunocompromised Hosts: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Oral step-down therapy for Gram-negative bacteremia is a key antimicrobial stewardship strategy, but landmark trials like the POET study excluded neutropenic and solid organ transplant patients. This leaves a critical evidence gap for managing bacteremia in these high-risk populations, who face prolonged IV therapy with associated risks of CLABSI, prolonged hospitalization, and resource use. Evaluating oral step-down in this context is essential to expand stewardship safely.
proposals.hypothesis: In immunocompromised patients (neutropenic or solid organ transplant recipients) with Gram-negative bacteremia from a urinary or biliary source, oral step-down therapy after initial clinical response is non-inferior to continued IV therapy for the composite outcome of 30-day mortality or bacteremia recurrence. We hypothesize that oral step-down will be associated with reduced hospital length of stay.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data. An electronic algorithm, adapted from prior work on vancomycin-AKI, will identify eligible patients with bacteremia, immunocompromised status, and source. The primary exposure is step-down to an oral fluoroquinolone or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole after ≥48 hours of IV therapy. We will use propensity score matching to balance groups and compare outcomes via multivariable regression. All Enterobacterales isolates will be tested for ESBL production, with susceptibility to cefepime and oral agents assessed.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comparative effectiveness data on oral step-down for bacteremia in a high-risk immunocompromised cohort, directly informing stewardship guidelines. It will generate hypotheses on risk factors for failure, such as ESBL colonization (extending prior colonization work), and create a replicable algorithm for identifying this patient subset in EHR data.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges the prevailing exclusion of immunocompromised hosts from oral step-down paradigms by applying rigorous, algorithm-driven methods to a population where clinical trial data is absent. It integrates Dr. Cosgrove's expertise in EHR algorithms, ESBL epidemiology, and outcomes research in high-risk patients.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of Oral Fluoroquinolones versus Oral Beta-Lactams for Step-Down Therapy in Patients with Bacteremia due to ESBL-Producing Enterobacterales
proposals.motivation: Step-down to oral therapy for bloodstream infections (BSI) reduces hospital length of stay and cost, but optimal oral regimens for infections caused by Extended-Spectrum β-Lactamase (ESBL)-producing organisms are unclear. Fluoroquinolones are commonly used but face rising resistance and safety concerns, while oral beta-lactams like cefdinir are often avoided despite in-vitro activity. A comparative effectiveness analysis is needed to guide clinical decision-making and antimicrobial stewardship.
proposals.hypothesis: For patients with ESBL-producing Enterobacterales bacteremia achieving clinical stability, step-down therapy with an oral beta-lactam (e.g., cefdinir) is non-inferior to an oral fluoroquinolone (e.g., ciprofloxacin) in terms of clinical cure, and is associated with a lower risk of subsequent Clostridioides difficile infection and fluoroquinolone-associated adverse events.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, propensity score-matched cohort study using electronic health record data from 2018-2025. The study population will be adults with monomicrobial bacteremia due to ESBL-producing Enterobacterales (identified via prior methods) who received definitive intravenous therapy (e.g., cefepime, carbapenem) and were stepped down to an oral fluoroquinolone or oral beta-lactam. The primary outcome is a composite of 30-day mortality, bacteremia recurrence, or rehospitalization. Secondary outcomes include C. difficile infection and acute kidney injury. We will adapt our previously validated electronic algorithm for vancomycin-associated AKI to identify nephrotoxicity and use natural language processing on clinical notes to enhance outcome capture.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comparative effectiveness data for two common oral step-down strategies in a high-risk population, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship guidelines. It will quantify the trade-offs between efficacy and toxicity, supporting more personalized, safer de-escalation. The findings will be critical for developing future interventional trials and electronic clinical decision support tools for step-down therapy.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has described ESBL colonization and IV therapy outcomes, the comparative effectiveness of specific oral agents for step-down in this population is unexplored. This proposal uniquely leverages the investigator's expertise in electronic phenotyping algorithms and ESBL epidemiology to address a critical gap in transition-of-care stewardship.

proposals.title: Emulating a Target Trial to Compare Oral Step-Down Therapy Strategies for Extended-Spectrum β-Lactamase-Producing Enterobacterales Bacteremia: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Oral step-down therapy for ESBL-producing Enterobacterales (ESBL-E) bacteremia is a common clinical strategy to facilitate early discharge and reduce costs, but optimal timing and agent selection remain uncertain due to significant confounding in observational studies. Traditional retrospective comparisons are biased by clinicians' decisions to step down based on unmeasured patient factors like clinical improvement, leading to immortal time bias and confounding by indication. A rigorous framework is needed to generate more reliable evidence to guide antimicrobial stewardship.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a target trial emulation framework to a retrospective cohort of patients with ESBL-E bacteremia will mitigate confounding and reveal a non-inferior clinical effectiveness (e.g., 30-day mortality, readmission) for early (≤4 days of IV therapy) versus later (>4 days) oral step-down with a highly bioavailable agent like cefepime (if susceptible) or a fluoroquinolone.
proposals.method: We will apply the target trial emulation methodology as formalized by Hernán and Robins. Using electronic health record data from high-risk patients with ESBL-E bacteremia (building on Cosgrove's prior colonization work), we will explicitly define eligibility criteria, treatment strategies, assignment procedures, outcomes, follow-up, and causal contrasts. We will use cloning, censoring, and weighting to emulate randomization and address immortal time bias. Comparative effectiveness will be estimated using inverse probability of treatment and censoring weighted (IPTW) survival models, with sensitivity analyses for unmeasured confounding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, bias-reduced estimate of the comparative effectiveness of early oral step-down for ESBL-E bacteremia, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship protocols. It will demonstrate the practical application of advanced causal inference methods to a critical infectious diseases question, setting a methodological standard for future observational studies of antibiotic therapy duration and route.
proposals.novelty: While target trial emulation is gaining traction in cardiology and oncology, its application to antimicrobial stewardship, particularly for ESBL-E infections, is novel. This study uniquely integrates this causal inference framework with deep clinical expertise in ESBL epidemiology and hospital-acquired infections to address a high-stakes, pragmatic treatment question.

proposals.title: Real-World Validation of Oral Step-Down Therapy for ESBL-Producing Enterobacterales Infections in Community Hospital and Outpatient Parenteral Antibiotic Therapy Settings
proposals.motivation: Research on oral step-down therapy for extended-spectrum β-lactamase-producing Enterobacterales (ESBL-E) infections has primarily been conducted in large academic centers, limiting its generalizability. Community hospitals and outpatient parenteral antibiotic therapy (OPAT) programs manage a significant proportion of these infections but face distinct challenges, including limited infectious diseases consultation and resource constraints. Demonstrating the safety and efficacy of oral step-down in these real-world settings is critical to broadening implementation and reducing the burdens of prolonged intravenous therapy and hospitalization.
proposals.hypothesis: A protocol-guided oral step-down strategy for ESBL-E bloodstream infections and complicated urinary tract infections, using agents like oral cefixime or fluoroquinolones based on susceptibility, will result in non-inferior clinical cure rates and reduced healthcare utilization compared to continued intravenous therapy in community hospital and OPAT settings.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, pragmatic, non-inferiority cohort study across a network of community hospitals and affiliated OPAT programs. Adult patients with ESBL-E bacteremia or cUTI who have received 3-5 days of effective IV therapy (e.g., cefepime, carbapenem) will be enrolled. An electronic algorithm, adapted from prior work on vancomycin-AKI, will screen electronic health records to identify eligible patients and prompt clinicians with a step-down decision support tool. The primary outcome is clinical cure at 30 days. Secondary outcomes include 30-day readmission, Clostridioides difficile infection, and total antibiotic days. We will compare outcomes to a historical cohort managed with standard IV therapy.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust, real-world evidence for the safety and effectiveness of oral step-down therapy for ESBL-E infections outside tertiary care centers. It will generate a validated, scalable protocol and electronic decision support tool tailored for resource-limited settings. The findings have direct potential to change practice, reduce OPAT complications and costs, and decrease hospital length of stay in community hospitals.
proposals.novelty: While oral step-down for ESBL-E is an emerging concept, its systematic evaluation has not been extended to the community hospital and OPAT environment where implementation barriers differ. This study uniquely integrates a pragmatic trial design with an electronic screening and decision support architecture, directly building on Dr. Cosgrove's prior work in algorithmic clinical support, to address a key translational gap.

proposals.title: Feasibility and Outcomes of Oral Step-Down Therapy for Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Acinetobacter baumannii Complex Bacteremia: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Oral step-down therapy for gram-negative bacteremia is a key strategy to reduce hospital length of stay and associated risks like CLABSI, but evidence is largely confined to ESBL-producing Enterobacterales. Pseudomonas and Acinetobacter are major non-fermenting pathogens with high morbidity, resistance concerns (e.g., to fluoroquinolones), and limited oral options, creating uncertainty about the safety of early transition. Evaluating this practice in these high-risk populations is critical to inform antimicrobial stewardship and patient safety initiatives.
proposals.hypothesis: In selected patients with Pseudomonas or Acinetobacter bacteremia, oral step-down therapy (e.g., with high-dose fluoroquinolones or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) will be non-inferior to continued intravenous therapy in terms of clinical cure and 30-day recurrence, but will be associated with a shorter hospital length of stay.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from 2018-2025. An electronic algorithm, building on prior work to identify vancomycin-associated AKI, will identify eligible bacteremia episodes from blood culture data and antibiotic administration records. The cohort will be stratified by pathogen (Pseudomonas vs. Acinetobacter) and oral step-down (exposed) vs. continued IV therapy (control). Primary outcomes are 30-day all-cause mortality and bacteremia recurrence. Multivariable regression and propensity score matching will adjust for confounders including source control, severity of illness, and isolate susceptibility (e.g., to cefepime).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, real-world evidence on the feasibility and outcomes of oral step-down for these challenging pathogens, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship guidelines. It will identify patient and pathogen characteristics (e.g., susceptibility profiles, source of infection) associated with successful oral transition, offering a data-driven framework for clinical decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While oral step-down for ESBL-E bacteremia is being actively studied, its application to non-fermenting gram-negative rods like Pseudomonas and Acinetobacter is largely unexplored and considered higher risk due to fewer oral options and perceived treatment complexity. This work tests the generalizability of the oral transition paradigm beyond Enterobacterales.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of an Electronic Decision Algorithm for an Oral-First Empiric Treatment Protocol for Community-Onset Bacteremia in Low-Resource Settings
proposals.motivation: In low-resource settings, sterile supplies and reliable intravenous (IV) access are scarce, increasing the risk of bloodstream infections (BSI) and complications like CLABSI from necessary IV lines. While oral-first protocols for bacteremia are being explored in high-income settings, there is no validated, context-specific framework to guide their safe and effective implementation where the burden of antimicrobial resistance (e.g., ESBL-producing Enterobacterales) is high and diagnostic capabilities are limited.
proposals.hypothesis: An electronic clinical decision algorithm, integrating local epidemiology of ESBL colonization, severity scoring, and oral bioavailability data, can identify a subset of patients with community-onset bacteremia for whom an oral-first empiric treatment protocol is non-inferior to standard IV therapy in terms of clinical cure and mortality.
proposals.method: We will adapt methodologies from our prior work on electronic AKI algorithms and pathogen exposure systems. First, a retrospective cohort study will analyze existing data from partner hospitals in low-resource settings to model risk factors for treatment failure. This will inform a rule-based electronic algorithm that inputs patient demographics, severity scores (e.g., qSOFA), source of infection, and prior ESBL colonization status (as per our prior research) to output a treatment pathway recommendation (IV vs. oral-first). The algorithm will then be piloted prospectively, using a modified Delphi process with local clinicians as advisors to ensure feasibility, with outcomes compared to a matched historical cohort.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first structured, electronic protocol for oral-first bacteremia management tailored to low-resource constraints. It will generate preliminary data on safety and efficacy, directly addressing a critical gap in global sepsis care. The deliverable will be a ready-to-implement algorithmic tool and a framework for antimicrobial stewardship in resource-limited environments.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely merges the concept of oral step-down therapy for bacteremia with the upfront ('oral-first') needs of low-resource settings, using an electronic decision support system informed by local ESBL colonization epidemiology rather than susceptibility testing, which is often unavailable.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Multi-Parameter Clinical Risk Score for Bacteremia in Afebrile, High-Risk Patients
proposals.motivation: Current clinical practice heavily relies on fever as the primary trigger for blood cultures, yet up to 30% of bacteremic patients, particularly the elderly, immunocompromised, or those with ESBL-producing organism colonization, present without fever. This leads to delayed diagnosis, inappropriate antibiotic use, and worse outcomes. A more sensitive, data-driven trigger is needed to improve early detection in these vulnerable populations.
proposals.hypothesis: A composite risk score integrating real-time vital sign trends, laboratory markers (e.g., lactate, bandemia, creatinine), and patient-specific factors (e.g., ESBL colonization status, recent antibiotic exposure) will more accurately predict bacteremia than fever alone in high-risk, afebrile patients.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from patients with suspected infection. Candidate predictors will include vital sign volatility (heart rate, respiratory rate), laboratory values, and risk factors from prior work on ESBL colonization. We will train a logistic regression model, with potential for a gradient boosting machine (e.g., XGBoost) for non-linear interactions, to predict positive blood cultures. Model performance will be validated against the standard fever-based trigger using area under the ROC curve and decision curve analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, actionable clinical decision support tool to guide blood culture acquisition, potentially increasing diagnostic yield in afebrile patients. It will advance the field of clinical microbiology by applying a systems biology framework to a fundamental diagnostic procedure, directly addressing a gap in care for patients at high risk for resistant organisms.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in its direct analogy to multi-parameter early warning systems from other fields and its specific focus on integrating colonization data (e.g., ESBL) and subtle physiological trends, moving beyond static, single-threshold triggers like fever.

proposals.title: Characterization of Afebrile Bacteremia Phenotypes Using Natural Language Processing to Optimize Blood Culture Indications
proposals.motivation: Fever is a primary trigger for blood culture, yet a significant proportion of bacteremia occurs in afebrile patients, leading to diagnostic delays and worse outcomes. Current clinical decision rules are largely based on structured data and may miss the nuanced clinical picture documented in provider notes. This gap is critical in high-risk populations, such as those with ESBL colonization or prior antibiotic exposure, where timely diagnosis is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying NLP to clinical notes will reveal distinct, quantifiable phenotypic profiles associated with bacteremia in afebrile patients, and that these profiles can be used to create a more sensitive and specific model for triggering blood cultures than fever alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of adult inpatients with positive blood cultures from 2018-2024. Using a pre-trained transformer model (e.g., ClinicalBERT or BioBERT) fine-tuned on our corpus, we will extract named entities for signs, symptoms, risk factors, and clinician suspicion from clinical notes in the 24 hours preceding culture. We will use unsupervised learning (e.g., latent class analysis) on these structured NLP outputs to identify distinct phenotypic clusters. These clusters will then be used as features to train a supervised model (e.g., XGBoost) to predict bacteremia, with performance validated against a held-out test set.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first data-driven, NLP-derived taxonomy of afebrile bacteremia phenotypes. It will deliver a validated, automated model that synthesizes unstructured clinical narrative to suggest patient-specific culture indications, moving beyond a reliance on fever. The findings could directly inform clinical decision support tools to improve early detection of bloodstream infections.
proposals.novelty: While NLP has been used for surveillance, its application to *discover* novel clinical phenotypes of bacteremia from free-text notes, particularly in afebrile patients, is novel. This approach fuses deep clinical adjudication with machine learning to create a new paradigm for culture triggers, distinct from prior rule-based or structured-data models.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Clinical Information Gain of Positive Blood Cultures: A Novel Framework to Distinguish Diagnostic Echoes from Practice-Changing Results
proposals.motivation: In the management of bloodstream infections, a positive blood culture is treated as a definitive event, yet its actual impact on clinical decision-making is highly variable. Many cultures merely confirm existing empirical therapy, consuming resources without altering care, while others are critical for de-escalation or targeted treatment. A framework to objectively measure the clinical value of each positive result is needed to optimize diagnostic stewardship and antimicrobial use, particularly in high-risk patients with complex infections like those caused by ESBL-producing organisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the clinical impact of a positive blood culture can be quantified as the information gain, measured by the divergence between pre-test and post-test therapeutic probability distributions, and that cultures with low information gain ('diagnostic echoes') are associated with specific clinical and microbiological profiles.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of patients with positive blood cultures at our institution. For each episode, we will use a structured data extraction tool to reconstruct the pre-test clinical scenario, including presentation, biomarkers, and local antibiogram data, to define a prior probability distribution for the causative pathogen and optimal therapy.
