Post-test management will be captured from the electronic record. Information gain will be calculated using the Kullback-Leibler divergence between these pre- and post-test distributions. We will then use multivariable regression to identify predictors (e.g., ESBL colonization status, source of infection) of low versus high information gain results.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, theory-grounded metric for the clinical utility of a positive blood culture. It will directly inform diagnostic stewardship by identifying patient and pathogen factors associated with low-yield cultures. The framework can be adapted for real-time clinical decision support, helping clinicians interpret culture results in the context of pre-test probability.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in applying formal information theory concepts to clinical microbiology diagnostics, moving beyond binary positivity to a continuous measure of diagnostic value. It directly challenges the assumed equivalence between a positive culture result and clinical impact, a paradigm not addressed by existing clinical prediction rules or antimicrobial stewardship tools.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Comorbidity-Stratified Risk Model for Gram-Negative Bacteremia in Non-Neutropenic Inpatients
proposals.motivation: Current infection prevention and antimicrobial stewardship strategies often treat non-neutropenic inpatients as a uniform risk group, despite evidence that specific comorbidities like diabetes, cirrhosis, and ESBL colonization dramatically increase susceptibility to bacteremia. This one-size-fits-all approach may misallocate resources and delay targeted interventions. A refined, risk-stratified model is needed to guide more precise surveillance, prophylaxis, and empirical therapy decisions in high-risk subpopulations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a predictive model incorporating specific comorbidities and prior colonization status with multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs) will significantly outperform a uniform-risk model in identifying non-neutropenic inpatients at highest risk for Gram-negative bacteremia.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of non-neutropenic adult inpatients (2018-2024). The primary outcome is hospital-onset bacteremia due to Enterobacterales or Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Predictors will include comorbidities (e.g., diabetes, cirrhosis, solid organ transplant), prior MDRO colonization (using an algorithm similar to prior work on ESBL-E), and recent antibiotic exposure (e.g., cefepime). We will develop a multivariable logistic regression model, with potential machine learning validation (e.g., random forest), to generate a risk score. Model performance will be assessed using area under the ROC curve (AUC) and calibration plots.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, electronic health record-embedded risk stratification tool to identify non-neutropenic patients at highest risk for Gram-negative bacteremia. It will directly inform targeted infection prevention bundles and guide the design of future stewardship trials for empirical or prophylactic therapy in defined high-risk cohorts, moving beyond neutropenia as the sole major risk paradigm.
proposals.novelty: While risk models exist for neutropenic or critically ill patients, this focuses on the heterogeneous general inpatient population, leveraging pre-2026 expertise in electronic phenotyping (e.g., for AKI, ESBL colonization) to create a novel, comorbidity-driven stratification specifically for Gram-negative bacteremia risk.

proposals.title: Modeling the Contamination Risk Niche: A Systematic Review and Ecological Framework for Predicting Blood Culture Contamination in Immunocompromised Inpatients
proposals.motivation: Blood culture contamination (BCC) in immunocompromised patients, particularly those with neutropenia or after hematopoietic cell transplant, leads to unnecessary antibiotic use, increased costs, and patient harm. Current literature is descriptive, lacking a predictive framework to identify high-risk scenarios. A structured, risk-stratified approach is urgently needed to improve diagnostic stewardship and patient safety in this vulnerable population.
proposals.hypothesis: The risk of blood culture contamination in immunocompromised hosts is not random but defines a predictable 'niche' characterized by specific, identifiable patient-level and system-level variables. These variables can be systematically mapped to create a predictive model analogous to ecological Species Distribution Models.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic scoping review following PRISMA-ScR guidelines. Extracted data on BCC events will be structured as 'presence' data. We will code associated variables into ecological analogs: patient factors (e.g., neutrophil count, GVHD grade, catheter type) as 'biotic' variables, and system factors (e.g., phlebotomy team, collection method, time of day) as 'abiotic' variables. Analysis will employ a meta-analytic framework and multivariate logistic regression models to identify the correlation strength and predictive power of these variables, creating a provisional 'risk niche' map.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first predictive, rather than descriptive, framework for understanding BCC in immunocompromised hosts. It will directly inform the development of risk-stratified phlebotomy protocols and targeted interventions. The resulting conceptual model will serve as a foundation for future validation studies and electronic clinical decision support tools.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct application of ecological niche modeling (SDM) concepts to a clinical microbiology problem. It moves beyond reporting incidence to structurally analyzing the multivariate 'environment' that defines contamination risk, a methodological leap not previously applied to this domain.

proposals.title: A Self-Calibrating Bayesian Network for Real-Time, Site-Specific Bacteremia Risk Prediction in High-Risk Patients
proposals.motivation: Accurate pre-test probability estimation for bacteremia is critical for timely, appropriate antibiotic initiation, especially in patients colonized with resistant organisms like ESBL-producing Enterobacterales. Current clinical prediction rules are static and do not adapt to local, evolving epidemiology or practice patterns, potentially leading to suboptimal diagnostic stewardship and antimicrobial use. A dynamic, learning system is needed to integrate real-time institutional data and improve decision support at the point of care.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical Bayesian network, continuously updated with local microbiology and patient outcome data, will provide more accurate and timely estimates of bacteremia probability than static models, and its performance can be maintained across a healthcare network through shared-strength learning.
proposals.method: We will develop a dynamic Bayesian network using a No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS) for inference, implemented in PyMC3 or Stan. Core nodes will include clinical syndromes (e.g., fever, hypotension), host factors, and prior colonization status with organisms like ESBL-E. The model will ingest live feeds from the EHR and microbiology (e.g., blood culture results, susceptibility patterns for cefepime). Prior probabilities and likelihood ratios will be updated daily via Markov Chain Monte Carlo. A hierarchical layer will allow parameter sharing across network hospitals, with automated auditing using statistical process control charts to flag prediction drift.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, adaptive clinical decision support tool that directly addresses the challenge of diagnostic stewardship in the era of antimicrobial resistance. It will demonstrate a novel methodology for creating 'learning health system' components that evolve with local epidemiology, potentially reducing unnecessary antibiotic use and improving outcomes for high-risk patients.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian networks exist in medicine, this approach uniquely integrates continuous, automated self-calibration with live data and a hierarchical structure for multi-site implementation, moving beyond a static model to a truly adaptive system that learns from its environment.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of an Electronic Clinical Decision Support Framework for Optimizing Blood Culture Collection in the Emergency Department
proposals.motivation: Inappropriate blood culture collection in the emergency department (ED) leads to false-positive results, unnecessary antibiotic use, and increased healthcare costs. Existing frameworks for optimizing blood culture use are designed for inpatient settings and do not account for the high diagnostic uncertainty, time pressure, and limited initial data characteristic of the ED. An ED-specific framework is urgently needed to improve diagnostic stewardship and patient outcomes at this critical care entry point.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning-based clinical decision support framework, trained on ED-specific clinical and laboratory data available at triage and initial evaluation, can accurately identify patients for whom blood cultures are indicated, reducing low-yield collections without missing true bloodstream infections.
proposals.method: We will adapt the electronic algorithm methodology used for vancomycin-associated acute kidney injury to the ED context. Using a retrospective cohort of ED encounters with linked blood culture results, we will train a gradient boosting model (XGBoost) on structured data available within the first hour of presentation (e.g., vital signs, chief complaint, past medical history, recent ESBL colonization status, initial labs). The algorithm will output a probabilistic indication score. We will validate the framework's performance in a prospective cohort, measuring its impact on culture collection rates, positivity rates, and time to appropriate therapy.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first validated, electronic framework for blood culture indication specifically designed for the ED workflow. It will contribute directly to antimicrobial and diagnostic stewardship by providing a scalable tool to reduce low-value testing. The framework will also generate novel data on predictors of bacteremia in the undifferentiated ED patient, including the role of prior multi-drug resistant organism colonization.
proposals.novelty: This project moves beyond inpatient-centric stewardship tools by explicitly designing for the unique constraints and data availability of the ED. It innovatively integrates known patient risk factors (e.g., prior ESBL colonization from Dr. Cosgrove's work) with real-time ED presentation data to create a dynamic, point-of-care decision aid, a significant advance over static inpatient checklists.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Clinical Risk-Priority Index for Blood Culture Allocation in Resource-Limited Settings
proposals.motivation: In resource-limited hospitals, blood culture capacity is a scarce diagnostic resource, leading to rationing without standardized guidance. This can delay appropriate antimicrobial therapy for critically ill patients with sepsis, while potentially wasting cultures on low-risk patients. A systematic, evidence-based triage protocol is urgently needed to optimize the clinical impact of limited microbiology resources, analogous to disaster triage systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A simple clinical risk-priority index, derived from readily available bedside parameters, can accurately stratify patients with suspected sepsis, and its implementation will safely direct limited blood cultures to a high-yield, high-risk patient subset without worsening clinical outcomes for those not prioritized.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-phase study. First, a retrospective cohort analysis of patients with suspected sepsis will identify independent predictors of positive blood culture and clinical deterioration to derive the index. Second, we will prospectively validate the index in a different cohort, measuring its sensitivity/specificity. The algorithm will assign a priority score based on parameters like hypotension (SBP<90), altered mental status, lactate >4 mmol/L, temperature >39°C, and recent ESBL colonization (leveraging Dr. Cosgrove's prior work). Patients scoring above a defined threshold will be allocated a blood culture; others will follow a standardized empirical management pathway.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated clinical tool for rational allocation of blood cultures in resource-constrained environments. It will offer a scalable framework for diagnostic stewardship that can be adapted globally. The research will also define safe alternative management protocols for low-priority patients, contributing to antimicrobial stewardship efforts.
proposals.novelty: This adapts the formal triage paradigm from emergency and disaster medicine—a novel conceptual framework—to the specific problem of diagnostic resource allocation in microbiology. It moves beyond simple prediction models for bacteremia to an operational protocol for rationing, integrating risk stratification with defined care pathways.

proposals.title: A Reinforcement Learning Framework for Personalized Cessation of Serial Blood Cultures in Patients with Gram-Negative Bacteremia
proposals.motivation: Serial blood cultures are a cornerstone of managing bloodstream infections but are resource-intensive and can lead to unnecessary antibiotic exposure and healthcare costs. Current guidelines for when to stop cultures are largely static and do not adapt to individual patient risk factors, such as ESBL colonization or source of infection. An adaptive, evidence-based policy is needed to balance the risk of undetected persistent bacteremia against the burdens of excessive testing.
proposals.hypothesis: A reinforcement learning agent, trained on retrospective EHR and microbiological data, can learn a dynamic policy for serial culture cessation that reduces unnecessary testing while maintaining or improving clinical outcomes compared to standard protocols.
proposals.method: We will develop a Markov Decision Process (MDP) model where the state includes patient demographics, infection source, prior culture results (including ESBL status), antibiotic therapy (e.g., cefepime), and clinical markers. A Deep Q-Network (DQN) agent will be trained on a large, retrospective cohort of patients with Gram-negative bacteremia. The reward function will penalize unnecessary cultures and adverse outcomes (e.g., recurrent bacteremia, mortality). The learned policy will be validated on a held-out temporal cohort.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first patient-optimized, adaptive decision model for serial culture stewardship. It will provide a proof-of-concept for using reinforcement learning to create dynamic diagnostic protocols in infectious diseases. The framework can be extended to other high-stakes, sequential diagnostic decisions in hospitalized patients.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used algorithms to detect adverse events (e.g., vancomycin-AKI), applying reinforcement learning to optimize the timing of a fundamental diagnostic test like blood culture cessation is novel. It moves beyond static risk prediction to sequential, personalized decision-making.

proposals.title: A Multi-Modal Electronic Intervention to Improve Adherence to Blood Culture Collection Volume Standards and Reduce Contamination
proposals.motivation: Inadequate blood culture collection volume is a major, under-recognized factor contributing to false-negative results and diagnostic delays in sepsis, directly impacting patient outcomes. Concurrently, high blood culture contamination rates, often linked to improper collection technique, lead to unnecessary antibiotic use, increased costs, and CLABSI-like diagnostic confusion. Pre-2026, most interventions target contamination alone, neglecting the critical variable of volume compliance.
proposals.hypothesis: A bundled electronic intervention, combining real-time feedback on collection volume with standardized collection protocol enforcement, will significantly improve adherence to optimal blood draw volumes and reduce contamination rates compared to standard practice.
proposals.method: We will develop and implement an electronic algorithm within the existing hospital EHR and specimen labeling system, modeled on prior work for vancomycin-AKI surveillance. The system will: 1) generate a real-time alert if an ordered blood culture is not paired with a corresponding order for the recommended blood volume collection kit; 2) integrate with barcode scanners at the point-of-care to verify kit use and prompt the collector to confirm volume; 3) provide immediate, personalized audit feedback to collectors via a dashboard. We will evaluate this using a stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial across hospital units, measuring pre- and post-intervention adherence (via kit scan logs) and contamination rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, data-driven model for improving a fundamental diagnostic metric in sepsis care. It will generate novel evidence linking standardized volume collection protocols to reduced contamination, offering a dual-benefit intervention. The findings will directly inform best practices for antimicrobial stewardship and diagnostic accuracy in bloodstream infections.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond passive education or contamination-focused alerts by creating an active, closed-loop electronic system that enforces volume compliance at the point of care, a novel integration not described in pre-2026 literature. It leverages the researcher's expertise in electronic clinical decision support algorithms for a new application in diagnostic stewardship.

proposals.title: A Clinical Decision Support Algorithm to Reduce Unnecessary Urine and Wound Cultures in the Emergency Department: A Stewardship Intervention
proposals.motivation: Diagnostic stewardship, proven to reduce unnecessary testing and antibiotic overuse in inpatient settings, is critically underdeveloped for high-volume, time-pressured environments like the Emergency Department (ED). Unnecessary cultures from the ED and outpatient clinics drive inappropriate antibiotic initiation, contribute to diagnostic cascades, and increase laboratory costs and workload. Extending stewardship principles to this front-line setting is essential to combat antimicrobial resistance and improve care quality.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing an electronic health record (EHR)-embedded clinical decision support algorithm, based on validated clinical and microbiologic criteria, will safely reduce the rate of low-yield urine and wound cultures ordered in the ED without adversely impacting patient outcomes.
proposals.method: We will develop and implement a rule-based algorithm within the Epic EHR using the BestPractice Advisory (BPA) framework. For urine cultures, the algorithm will integrate criteria such as absence of urinary symptoms, lack of leukocyte esterase/nitrites on urinalysis, and prior culture history (including ESBL colonization status from prior work). For wound cultures, it will assess signs of systemic infection and prior antimicrobial therapy. The intervention will be evaluated using a stepped-wedge, cluster-randomized design across multiple EDs, comparing culture rates, antibiotic days of therapy, and 30-day return visits pre- and post-implementation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, real-world model for extending diagnostic stewardship beyond the hospital floor. It will generate evidence on the feasibility and impact of automated stewardship tools in the ED, a major source of antibiotic prescriptions. The study will also contribute data on the colonization-to-infection pipeline for multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs) in ambulatory patients.
proposals.novelty: While inpatient diagnostic stewardship is established, this adapts and rigorously tests these principles for the unique workflow and patient population of the ED. It leverages Dr. Cosgrove's expertise in electronic algorithm development (e.g., for vancomycin-AKI) and MDRO colonization (ESBL work) to create a novel, integrated intervention.

proposals.title: Genomic Epidemiology of ESBL-Producing Enterobacterales Reveals Community and Healthcare Reservoirs Driving Hospital-Onset Colonization in High-Risk Patients
proposals.motivation: Hospital infection control programs focus on intra-facility transmission, but antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is a One Health issue. Pre-2026 evidence shows high community carriage of Extended-Spectrum β-Lactamase (ESBL)-producing organisms, and agricultural use of antibiotics drives resistance. It is unknown what proportion of hospital-onset ESBL colonization in high-risk patients originates from community or environmental reservoirs versus hospital transmission, limiting the effectiveness of control strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: A significant proportion of hospital-onset gastrointestinal colonization with ESBL-producing Enterobacterales in high-risk patients is caused by strains genetically linked to community or agricultural reservoirs, not by recent hospital-based transmission.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of high-risk patients (e.g., ICU, oncology) screened for ESBL colonization on admission and weekly. Using whole-genome sequencing (WGS) of patient isolates (rectal swabs), we will apply core-genome multilocus sequence typing (cgMLST) and phylogenetic analysis to compare strains from patients who develop hospital-onset colonization to: 1) a pre-existing hospital isolate biobank, 2) a curated database of community-associated ESBL genomes from local outpatient clinics, and 3) publicly available genomic data from agricultural and environmental sources. An electronic algorithm, modeled on Cosgrove's prior AKI work, will automate the identification of 'hospital-onset colonization' events and trigger isolate collection for sequencing.
proposals.contribution: This study will quantify the genomic linkage of hospital-onset ESBL strains to non-hospital reservoirs, providing direct evidence to challenge the sufficiency of hospital-centric control. It will generate a novel framework for integrating community genomic surveillance into hospital infection prevention risk assessments. Findings will inform public health policy on the need for integrated AMR containment across healthcare, community, and agricultural sectors.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Cosgrove et al. identified which ESBLs colonize high-risk patients, this study uniquely applies high-resolution genomics to trace the origin of hospital-onset colonization events beyond the hospital walls, a direct test of a foundational assumption in infection control.

proposals.title: A Community-Based Metagenomic Surveillance Platform for Early Detection of Emerging Extended-Spectrum β-Lactamase Genes
proposals.motivation: Hospital-based antimicrobial resistance (AMR) surveillance, while critical, is reactive, detecting resistance only after it has entered the healthcare system. The community gut microbiome is a known reservoir for ESBL-producing Enterobacterales, but scalable, longitudinal monitoring is lacking. Early detection of emerging resistance determinants in community populations could inform pre-emptive infection control and stewardship strategies before hospital transmission occurs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted metagenomic sequencing of community wastewater and volunteer stool samples will identify emerging ESBL gene patterns and plasmid backbones not yet prevalent in clinical isolates, providing an early warning signal for hospital-acquired infections.
proposals.method: We will recruit a community cohort (n=200) and collect serial stool samples over 12 months, alongside weekly wastewater sampling from targeted neighborhood sewersheds. DNA will be extracted and subjected to shotgun metagenomic sequencing. We will adapt our existing electronic algorithm for Vancomycin-AKI to create a bioinformatics pipeline that flags samples with high abundance of ESBL genes (e.g., blaCTX-M, blaSHV, blaTEM) and uses k-mer-based assembly to reconstruct resistance plasmids. Prevalence will be compared to a parallel clinical isolate biobank from our hospital (ESBL-E, cefepime-resistant).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept framework for community-driven AMR surveillance, moving beyond point-prevalence studies. It will generate a novel dataset linking environmental, community, and clinical resistance reservoirs, specifically for ESBLs. The findings will provide actionable data for public health advisors and infection prevention consultants to design targeted interventions.
proposals.novelty: While hospital ESBL surveillance is common, this proposal scales down surveillance to the community microbiome using a dual-sample strategy (wastewater and individual stools) to trace resistance flux. It uniquely integrates our group's expertise in electronic clinical algorithms with wet-lab metagenomics to create an automated early-detection system for community AMR.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Reversal of ESBL Colonization in High-Risk Patient Cohorts Following a Structured Antibiotic Withdrawal Protocol
proposals.motivation: The persistence of extended-spectrum β-lactamase-producing Enterobacterales (ESBL-E) in the gut of high-risk patients drives subsequent infections and transmission, complicating infection control. While antimicrobial stewardship focuses on reducing new resistance, the potential for reversing established colonization through targeted antibiotic withdrawal remains an underexplored inverse problem with significant implications for patient-level decolonization strategies and population-level resistance dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, systematic withdrawal of specific antibiotic classes (e.g., cephalosporins, fluoroquinolones) in a defined patient cohort will lead to a measurable decrease in the prevalence and genetic diversity of gastrointestinal ESBL-E colonization over time, indicating a reversal of established resistance reservoirs.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational cohort study in high-risk patient units (e.g., ICU, hematology-oncology) with known ESBL-E colonization. Leveraging Dr. Cosgrove's expertise in electronic algorithms and pathogen exposure tracking, we will implement an electronic health record-based algorithm to identify eligible patients and monitor strict withdrawal of target antibiotics. Serial rectal swabs will be collected weekly for 8 weeks. Isolates will undergo whole-genome sequencing to track clonal dynamics and resistance gene carriage (e.g., blaCTX-M). Statistical analysis will model time-to-clearance and changes in microbial diversity using mixed-effects models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first longitudinal, genomic evidence for the feasibility of reversing ESBL-E colonization in patients via antibiotic withdrawal. It will generate a novel, data-driven framework for 'resistance de-escalation' protocols, directly informing stewardship programs beyond mere prevention. The findings could redefine endpoints for patient decolonization and offer a new strategy to reduce the reservoir for healthcare-associated infections like CLABSI caused by multi-drug resistant organisms.
proposals.novelty: While stewardship reduces selective pressure, actively testing withdrawal as an intervention to reverse established colonization in individual patients is a novel inversion of the traditional resistance paradigm. The integration of real-time electronic algorithm-driven patient identification with genomic surveillance of resistance reversal moves beyond observational ecology to a testable clinical intervention.

proposals.title: Targeted Treatment of Catheter-Associated Asymptomatic Bacteriuria in High-Risk Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplant Recipients: A Retrospective Cohort Study to Assess Impact on Subsequent Bacteremia and Mortality
proposals.motivation: Current IDSA guidelines strongly recommend against treating catheter-associated asymptomatic bacteriuria (CA-ASB) to avoid antibiotic overuse and resistance. However, this recommendation is based largely on data from general populations. For profoundly immunocompromised patients, like hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT) recipients, untreated CA-ASB may serve as a reservoir for subsequent bloodstream infection (BSI), a major cause of mortality. Pre-2026, the risk-benefit calculus for this specific subgroup remains undefined.
proposals.hypothesis: In adult HSCT recipients with indwelling urinary catheters, targeted treatment of CA-ASB caused by extended-spectrum beta-lactamase (ESBL)-producing or other multidrug-resistant Enterobacterales will be associated with a reduced incidence of subsequent bacteremia from the same organism, without a significant increase in adverse drug events like acute kidney injury.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from 2018-2024. An electronic algorithm, building on Dr. Cosgrove's prior work, will identify HSCT recipients with CA-ASB (positive urine culture without UTI symptoms) and an indwelling catheter. The cohort will be stratified by treatment (antibiotics active against the isolate, e.g., cefepime for non-ESBL, carbapenems for ESBL) versus no treatment. The primary outcome will be bacteremia with the same organism within 30 days. Secondary outcomes include all-cause mortality, Clostridioides difficile infection, and vancomycin-associated acute kidney injury. Multivariable regression will adjust for neutropenia severity, graft-versus-host disease, and other confounders.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-quality evidence to guide the management of CA-ASB in a uniquely vulnerable population. It will challenge the blanket 'do not treat' paradigm by identifying a potential high-risk subgroup where pre-emptive therapy may be beneficial, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship and infection prevention practices in transplant centers.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond the established harm of overtreatment in general populations to investigate a potential, context-specific benefit in immunocompromised hosts, leveraging detailed electronic phenotyping and resistance profiling (ESBL) to define a precise at-risk cohort.

proposals.title: A Risk-Benefit Framework for Indwelling Urinary Catheters: Identifying Patient Subgroups Where Avoidance May Increase Harm
proposals.motivation: Current infection prevention efforts, including CLABSI and CAUTI bundles, strongly emphasize minimizing catheter use. However, this one-size-fits-all approach may inadvertently cause harm in specific patient populations where frequent catheterization for output monitoring or retention relief is clinically necessary, leading to trauma, discomfort, and iatrogenic infections from repeated insertions. A data-driven method to identify patients for whom an indwelling catheter presents a net benefit is critically needed to move beyond blanket avoidance policies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distinct subgroup of high-risk inpatients exists for whom the cumulative risk of harm from repeated intermittent catheterization exceeds the risk of a single indwelling catheter-associated urinary tract infection (CAUTI).
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from 2018-2024. The primary outcome is a composite harm measure (trauma, discomfort events, UTIs from intermittent cath, falls related to toileting). We will develop and validate a multivariable logistic regression model, informed by prior work on electronic AKI algorithms, to predict this composite harm. The model will incorporate variables like neurologic impairment, diuretic use, critical illness, and mobility status. We will then create a decision curve analysis to compare the net benefit of a model-guided catheter strategy versus universal avoidance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, patient-specific risk-benefit framework for urinary catheter decision-making, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship and infection prevention guidelines. It will shift the clinical paradigm from universal minimization to optimized, personalized use, potentially reducing overall patient harm.
proposals.novelty: This study inverts the standard CAUTI prevention question, focusing not on the risk of the device itself, but on the risk of *not* using it in specific contexts, a perspective largely absent from pre-2026 literature. It leverages Dr. Cosgrove's expertise in electronic phenotyping and ESBL epidemiology to model a complex clinical trade-off.

proposals.title: Adaptation and Feasibility Assessment of a CAUTI Prevention Bundle for Low-Resource Hospitals: A Mixed-Methods Implementation Study
proposals.motivation: Catheter-associated urinary tract infections (CAUTIs) are a major source of preventable morbidity and mortality globally, with a disproportionate burden in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) where resources for infection prevention are constrained. Existing evidence-based CAUTI prevention bundles, developed in high-resource settings, are often infeasible to implement in LMIC hospitals due to differences in staffing, supply chains, and infrastructure. Adapting these strategies to local contexts is a critical, unmet need to reduce healthcare-associated infections and their associated antimicrobial resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: A contextually adapted CAUTI prevention bundle, co-designed with local stakeholders, will be feasible to implement and will lead to a measurable reduction in catheter utilization rates and surrogate markers of CAUTI risk in low-resource hospital settings.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods, multi-phase approach. First, we will conduct formative qualitative interviews and workflow observations at 2-3 partner hospitals in a LMIC to identify barriers and facilitators to existing CAUTI prevention guidelines. Second, we will adapt a core bundle (e.g., nurse-driven catheter removal, aseptic insertion checklist, alternatives to indwelling catheters) using a structured adaptation framework. Third, we will implement the adapted bundle using a low-cost, paper-based or simple electronic audit and feedback system, modeled on principles from Dr. Cosgrove's prior work on electronic AKI algorithms and exposure response systems. Feasibility will be assessed via process measures, and impact will be evaluated via pre/post analysis of catheter device-days and clinically diagnosed CAUTI events.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, context-specific CAUTI prevention protocol ready for broader evaluation in LMICs. It will contribute a methodological framework for adapting other infection prevention strategies (e.g., CLABSI, ESBL colonization management) to low-resource settings. The findings will directly inform global health policy and practical implementation guides for hospital administrators in resource-limited environments.
proposals.novelty: While CAUTI prevention is well-studied, this proposal uniquely focuses on the systematic adaptation and feasibility testing of bundles *for* low-resource settings, moving beyond simply documenting gaps. It leverages Dr. Cosgrove's expertise in electronic clinical decision support and pathogen exposure systems to create simplified, sustainable tools rather than assuming high-resource digital infrastructure.

proposals.title: Prevalence and Genomic Epidemiology of Multidrug-Resistant Enterococcus, Candida, and Clostridioides difficile Among High-Risk Inpatients: Expanding the Pathogen Surveillance Portfolio
proposals.motivation: Current surveillance for multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs) in high-risk patients often focuses on a limited panel of Gram-negative bacteria, as evidenced by Dr. Cosgrove's prior work on ESBL-producing Enterobacterales. This leaves significant gaps in understanding the colonization burden of other high-consequence pathogens, including vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus (VRE), fluconazole-resistant Candida species, and toxigenic Clostridioides difficile, which are major drivers of healthcare-associated infections and antimicrobial use. A comprehensive, multi-pathogen prevalence survey is needed to inform targeted infection prevention strategies and antimicrobial stewardship, particularly in units with high rates of device use and antibiotic exposure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that active surveillance rectal and skin swab cultures, augmented by selective chromogenic media and PCR, will reveal a high prevalence of co-colonization with VRE, resistant Candida spp., and C. difficile among high-risk inpatients, and that whole-genome sequencing will identify patient-specific clusters and potential transmission networks not captured by routine clinical cultures.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, cross-sectional prevalence survey in high-risk units (e.g., ICU, hematology-oncology). Perirectal and inguinal swabs will be collected from consented patients. Specimens will be processed using a tiered algorithm: 1) ChromID VRE and chromogenic Candida agar for selective isolation; 2) PCR for *tcdB* gene (C. difficile) and *vanA/vanB* genes from enrichment broths; 3) Antimicrobial susceptibility testing for isolates. All MDRO isolates (ESBL-E, VRE, Candida, C. difficile) from a patient will undergo whole-genome sequencing (Illumina platform). Bioinformatic analysis will use established pipelines (e.g., SRST2 for gene detection, SNIPPY for core-genome phylogeny) to characterize resistance determinants and assess genetic relatedness.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first integrated datasets on the concurrent gastrointestinal colonization burden of key bacterial and fungal MDROs in a high-risk inpatient cohort. It will directly extend the researcher's previous ESBL prevalence work into a multi-kingdom surveillance model. The genomic data will elucidate the population structure of these pathogens and identify potential silent transmission events, offering actionable insights for infection prevention bundles beyond contact isolation.
proposals.novelty: While surveillance for individual MDROs is common, the simultaneous, algorithm-driven detection and genomic comparison of priority Gram-negative, Gram-positive, and fungal pathogens from the same patient samples is novel. This approach moves beyond siloed prevalence studies to create a holistic 'colonization profile,' enabling analysis of interspecies dynamics and shared risk factors within the microbiome of high-risk patients.

proposals.title: Disentangling Antibiotic Exposure from Infection Burden: A Causal Inference Analysis of Colorectal Cancer Risk in High-Risk Patient Cohorts
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 epidemiological studies consistently link antibiotic use to increased colorectal cancer (CRC) risk, but this association may be confounded. Antibiotics are prescribed for infections, and the underlying infection or the resultant inflammatory state, not the drug itself, could be the true driver of carcinogenesis. This distinction is critical for developing accurate risk models and informing clinical guidelines.
proposals.hypothesis: The association between antibiotic exposure and increased CRC risk is not a direct causal relationship but is primarily mediated by the burden and severity of the bacterial infections for which the antibiotics were prescribed.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from high-risk patients (e.g., those with prior ESBL colonization, CLABSI, or recurrent infections). We will develop and apply an electronic phenotyping algorithm, building on Cosgrove's prior work, to classify infection episodes (type, severity, pathogen) and antibiotic exposures. Using causal inference methods (e.g., marginal structural models or targeted maximum likelihood estimation), we will estimate the direct effect of antibiotic exposure versus the effect of the infection burden on subsequent CRC diagnosis, adjusting for time-varying confounders.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more nuanced, causal understanding of the antibiotic-CRC risk association, moving beyond simple exposure metrics. It will deliver a validated framework for separating drug effects from indication effects in oncological epidemiology. The findings could redirect preventive focus towards infection mitigation strategies in high-risk groups.
proposals.novelty: While prior work establishes correlation, this study explicitly models infection as a competing causal pathway using advanced causal methods from a clinical informatics perspective. It leverages detailed, algorithmically derived infection phenotypes rather than relying solely on antibiotic prescription records as a proxy.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Algorithm to Differentiate Sepsis from Non-Infectious SIRS in High-Risk Patients at Initial Presentation
proposals.motivation: Early, accurate sepsis diagnosis is critical for timely antimicrobial therapy but remains a major clinical challenge. The systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) criteria are non-specific, leading to over-diagnosis of sepsis, unnecessary antibiotic use, and potential patient harm. This problem is especially acute in high-risk patient populations, such as those colonized with multidrug-resistant organisms like ESBL-producing Enterobacterales, where inappropriate empiric therapy carries significant risk.
proposals.hypothesis: A machine learning model, trained on comprehensive electronic health record data available within the first hour of presentation, can reliably distinguish sepsis from non-infectious SIRS with greater accuracy than clinician judgment or standard screening tools like qSOFA.
proposals.method: We will develop a retrospective cohort of high-risk patients (e.g., ICU transfers, immunocompromised) presenting with SIRS. The outcome will be adjudicated sepsis (positive microbiologic culture + treatment) vs. non-infectious SIRS. Using methods from our prior work on electronic AKI algorithms, we will engineer features from structured EHR data (vitals, labs, demographics, prior ESBL colonization status, recent procedures). We will train and validate several models, including logistic regression, random forests, and gradient boosting machines, using a temporal split to ensure generalizability.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, electronic algorithm for early sepsis identification tailored to high-risk populations. It will provide quantitative evidence of the limitations of clinical judgment at presentation and establish a framework for future prospective intervention studies. The findings will directly inform antimicrobial stewardship and early resuscitation efforts.
proposals.novelty: While many sepsis prediction models exist, this proposal uniquely focuses on the critical, initial diagnostic dilemma at presentation, rather than deterioration in already-ill inpatients. It leverages the investigator's expertise in electronic phenotyping and high-risk patient cohorts (e.g., ESBL colonization) to build a more context-aware model.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of an Electronic Algorithm for Generating Hospital-Specific, Adaptive Sepsis Management Bundles
proposals.motivation: Sepsis management guidelines, such as the Surviving Sepsis Campaign bundles, are one-size-fits-all and may not account for critical local factors like antimicrobial resistance (e.g., ESBL prevalence), patient comorbidities, and institutional diagnostic capabilities. This mismatch can delay effective therapy and worsen outcomes. Dr. Cosgrove's prior work on electronic algorithms for adverse event detection and ESBL colonization epidemiology provides a unique foundation to address this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: An electronic algorithm that dynamically integrates local, real-time data on antimicrobial resistance patterns, patient risk factors, and institutional resources can generate adaptive, hospital-specific sepsis bundles that are associated with improved time-to-appropriate therapy and reduced mortality compared to static guideline-based bundles.
proposals.method: We will develop a rules-based electronic algorithm within the hospital EHR, building on the architecture of prior work (e.g., the vancomycin-AKI algorithm). The algorithm will integrate data streams from: 1) the clinical microbiology lab (local antibiograms, ESBL/carbapenemase rates from isolates), 2) patient-specific risk factors (prior colonization, comorbidities), and 3) institutional formulary and rapid diagnostic capabilities. For a given sepsis alert, it will output a prioritized, institution-specific bundle recommending first-line antimicrobials (e.g., guiding cefepime use based on local ESBL prevalence), diagnostics, and timing. We will validate the algorithm using a quasi-experimental, pre-post study in high-risk units, comparing process and clinical outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, data-driven framework for personalizing sepsis care at the institutional level, moving beyond generic guidelines. It will directly translate Dr. Cosgrove's expertise in electronic clinical decision support and antimicrobial resistance epidemiology into an actionable intervention designed to improve antibiotic appropriateness and patient outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While electronic sepsis alerts exist, they do not dynamically adapt recommendations based on a hospital's unique resistance ecology and resources. This approach integrates the 'advisor/consultant' function into an automated system, creating adaptive bundles that are both patient- and institution-specific, a logical extension of the researcher's work on pathogen exposure and high-risk patient colonization.

ideas.title: Applying 'Planetary Protection' Biocontainment Logic to Antibiotic Stewardship in Viral Pneumonia
ideas.description: This idea challenges the default practice of empiric antibiotics in COVID-19 and other viral pneumonias by drawing an analogy from planetary protection protocols in space science. In that field, the problem of preventing microbial contamination of other celestial bodies is not solved by indiscriminate pre-launch sterilization, but through meticulous assay to establish a bioburden baseline, followed by risk-tiered, targeted containment measures. Adapting this solution, the research proposes a paradigm shift in clinical management: mandating rapid, comprehensive pathogen detection (e.g., metagenomic sequencing) prior to antibiotic initiation in viral pneumonia patients. This 'pre-therapy assay' replaces the assumption of bacterial co-infection with an evidence-based 'infection burden' profile. Antibiotics are then deployed not empirically, but only as a 'targeted containment' strategy against specifically identified bacterial pathogens, mirroring how spacecraft components are selectively sterilized based on their destination and assay results. This approach directly challenges the foundational assumption that bacterial co-infection is probable enough to warrant blanket antimicrobial 'contamination' of the patient and environment.

ideas.title: Antimicrobial Stewardship via Predictive Resource Allocation Models from Supply Chain Logistics
ideas.description: This idea applies a solution from supply chain logistics—dynamic predictive resource allocation—to the problem of antimicrobial stewardship in COVID-19 admissions. In logistics, algorithms forecast demand and optimize inventory to prevent shortages and overstocking. Adapted to a clinical context, the system would treat antibiotics as a finite resource and patient infection risk as variable demand. By integrating real-time data on patient status, local epidemiology, and hospital antibiotic usage, it would generate stewardship decisions that dynamically allocate the most effective antibiotic therapy to the patients at highest risk while conserving broader antibiotic utility, effectively creating a logistical CDS for antimicrobial preservation.

ideas.title: NLP-driven discovery of non-febrile bacteremia phenotypes to refine culture triggers
ideas.description: This research fuses the critical challenge to fever-based diagnostic triggers with NLP-based adjudication to create a novel discovery pipeline. We will apply advanced NLP to a large retrospective cohort of bacteremic patients, extracting and quantifying the full spectrum of documented signs, symptoms, risk factors, and clinician suspicions from clinical notes, regardless of fever status. The system will then use this rich, unstructured data to identify and characterize distinct phenotypic profiles associated with bacteremia in afebrile and febrile patients. The output is a validated, automated model that can suggest patient-specific indications for blood culture based on a comprehensive, data-derived phenotype, fundamentally shifting the paradigm from a single vital sign to a multifaceted clinical picture.

ideas.description: This research adapts the framework of emergency triage from disaster medicine to solve the problem of rationing scarce blood culture capacity in resource-limited hospitals. Analogous to how START triage uses rapid clinical assessments to prioritize casualty care, this idea proposes developing and validating a clinical risk-priority index for suspected sepsis. The index would use readily available parameters (e.g., temperature, hypotension, mental status, lactate if available) to stratify patients, directing the limited blood cultures to a prioritized subset where results are most critical for guiding therapy, while defining alternative management pathways for others, thereby scaling diagnostic resources in a dual system of prioritized and standard care.

ideas.title: A Multi-Parameter Trigger for Blood Cultures: A Systems Biology Approach
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that fever is the primary and sufficient trigger for obtaining blood cultures, given that many bacteremic patients present without fever. Drawing an analogy from Earthquake Early Warning Systems, which moved from relying on single precursors to integrating multiple data streams into a composite risk model, we propose a similar shift in clinical practice. The adapted research idea is to develop and validate a multi-parameter clinical risk score that integrates real-time vital sign trends, laboratory markers, and patient-specific factors to generate a probability of bacteremia. This systems biology approach would trigger blood cultures based on a threshold of this composite score, rather than the presence or absence of a single symptom like fever, thereby improving the sensitivity and rationale for culturing in afebrile at-risk patients.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Modeling to Map Blood Culture Contamination Risk in Immunocompromised Hosts
ideas.description: This scoping review expansion for neutropenic and immunocompromised inpatients is reconceptualized by drawing an analogy from ecological niche modeling. In ecology, Species Distribution Models (SDMs) predict where a species is likely to occur by analyzing the correlation between known presence data and key environmental variables. This solution is adapted to the clinical problem by modeling the 'contamination risk niche' for blood cultures. The review will systematically map not just the incidence of contamination in this population, but will structure the analysis to identify the key patient-level (e.g., neutrophil count trajectory, presence of GVHD, catheter type) and system-level variables that define high-risk scenarios. The outcome is a predictive, rather than purely descriptive, framework that can inform the development of risk-stratified phlebotomy and culture protocols tailored to specific immunocompromised host 'niches'.

ideas.title: Dynamic, Patient-Optimized Policy for Serial Culture Cessation Using Reinforcement Learning
ideas.description: This project develops a data-driven, adaptive decision model to determine the optimal time to discontinue serial blood cultures for individual patients. Instead of static rules, a reinforcement learning agent is trained on retrospective electronic health record and microbiological data to learn a policy that sequences culture decisions based on evolving patient states. The model explicitly optimizes for the dual objectives of minimizing diagnostic resource use and avoiding adverse outcomes from undetected persistent bacteremia. This creates a personalized, evidence-based guideline that dynamically adjusts to a patient's unique clinical trajectory.

ideas.title: The Diagnostic Echo: Measuring the True Impact of a Positive Blood Culture by Assessing Information Gain
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a positive blood culture inherently signifies clinical impact by drawing an analogy from information theory and signal processing. In those fields, the value of a new signal is measured by the 'information gain' or reduction in uncertainty it provides relative to the prior state. Adapting this solution, the research would develop a framework to quantify the information gain of a positive blood culture result. This involves capturing the pre-test clinical probability distributions for pathogen and optimal therapy (based on presentation, biomarkers, and local epidemiology) and then measuring the divergence to the post-test plan. The outcome is a continuous or categorical measure of a culture's true clinical impact, distinguishing results that merely confirm existing therapy (low information gain, 'diagnostic echo') from those that fundamentally alter management (high information gain).

ideas.title: Adapting Phylogenetic Calibration Methods to Validate Co-infection Definitions Across Variants and Populations
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from evolutionary biology, where phylogenetic models are calibrated for new lineages. The analogous problem is validating a classification system (the consensus co-infection definitions) when applied to new evolutionary variants (later SARS-CoV-2 variants) and different populations (various geographic patient groups). The solution involves applying the existing definitions as a baseline model to retrospective and prospective data from these new contexts. By quantitatively assessing their performance against gold-standard microbiological data and clinical outcomes—similar to testing a molecular clock's fit—the research can determine if the definitions hold universally or require calibration (e.g., adjusting cycle threshold value cut-offs for PCR tests or syndromic criteria) to ensure accurate and consistent identification of co-infections across the evolving pandemic landscape.

ideas.title: Dynamic, Self-Calibrating Bayesian Network for Real-Time Bacteremia Probability Estimation
ideas.description: This system creates an adaptive clinical decision support tool that estimates the pre-test probability of bacteremia by integrating a dynamic Bayesian network with live institutional data feeds. The model's core parameters—prior probabilities and likelihood ratios for clinical syndrome combinations—are continuously updated using local microbiology results and patient outcomes, ensuring they reflect current epidemiology and practice. It employs a hierarchical structure to share strength across healthcare networks while preserving site-specific accuracy, and includes automated performance auditing to flag when predictions deviate from observed outcomes. This transforms a static diagnostic framework into a learning health system component that evolves with the clinical environment.

ideas.title: Fairness-Aware ML for Equitable Bacteremia Prediction and Blood Culture Guidance
ideas.description: This research develops a machine learning system for predicting bacteremia from EHR data that is explicitly designed to mitigate and correct for biases in historical clinical decision-making. The model architecture incorporates adversarial debiasing and fairness constraints during training to ensure its predictions do not perpetuate disparities in care based on protected attributes such as race, ethnicity, or insurance status. It is rigorously validated across diverse patient subgroups to guarantee equitable performance, aiming to reduce unnecessary testing in over-served populations while improving detection in under-tested groups. The final output provides a clinically actionable risk score alongside a bias audit report to support equitable stewardship of blood culture ordering.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Aware NLP Pre-Adjudication Assistant with Human-in-the-Loop Integration
ideas.description: This system uses NLP to extract and structure potential evidence for co-infection adjudication from clinical notes but crucially focuses on identifying and quantifying gaps, contradictions, and ambiguities in the textual evidence. It generates an integrated case summary with highlighted evidence, assigns a confidence score based on data completeness and consistency, and explicitly flags high-uncertainty elements. The output is designed for efficient human review, where the adjudicator makes the final decision using the system's analysis as a structured starting point, thereby augmenting human judgment rather than attempting to replace it.

ideas.title: Clinical Adjudication as a Bayesian Prior: A Seismology-Informed Framework for Diagnosing Infectious Co-Infections
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that microbiologic testing alone suffices for diagnosing co-infections by formally adapting a solution from seismology. In earthquake detection, raw seismographic data is insufficient to distinguish a mainshock-aftershock sequence from independent events; analysts adjudicate by integrating contextual geologic data. Analogously, in infectious diseases, the mere microbiologic detection of multiple pathogens is a 'raw signal' that overestimates clinically relevant co-infection. The adapted research idea is to create a clinical adjudication framework that uses Bayesian priors—derived from host factors, syndromic presentation, and epidemiological context—to weight the microbiologic data. This generates a probability score for causative co-infection, transforming diagnosis from a microbiology-only binary to a integrated probabilistic model, thereby refining prevalence estimates and treatment thresholds.

ideas.title: Longitudinal co-infection tracking via serial multi-omic sampling and ecological modeling
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by using an analogy from ecology. In environmental science, understanding ecosystem disturbance and succession requires longitudinal sampling and dynamic modeling. Adapted to COVID-19 hospitalization, the research would track bacterial co-infection development not just at admission but via serial, multi-omic sampling (e.g., metagenomics, transcriptomics from respiratory, blood samples) at regular intervals throughout the hospital stay. This longitudinal data would be analyzed with ecological models (e.g., microbial community dynamics, host-pathogen interaction networks) to identify the temporal trajectories, predictive biomarkers, and clinical triggers of nosocomial superinfections, moving from snapshot detection to a predictive, process-oriented understanding.

ideas.title: Adaptive Early Warning System for Bacteremia Using Sequential Data and Ensemble Methods
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from financial fraud detection to the problem of predicting bacteremia from EHR data. In fraud detection, ensemble models analyze sequences of transactions to generate a dynamic, updated risk score, using cost-sensitive learning to balance missed fraud against false alarms. The adapted research idea applies this by treating a patient's longitudinal EHR data as a time-ordered sequence of clinical events. An ensemble machine learning model (e.g., Gradient Boosting or Random Forest) will be developed to consume this sequential data, outputting a continuously updated probability of bacteremia. The model will be trained with a cost-sensitive objective function that heavily penalizes false negatives (missed bacteremia) while managing false positives (unnecessary cultures), thereby creating an adaptive clinical decision support tool to guide blood culture ordering with improved accuracy and clinical utility.

This dual-purpose design enables the system to generate real-time, comparable global co-infection burden estimates while simultaneously assessing the robustness and necessary adaptations of the definitions themselves. The resulting feedback loop creates a living surveillance framework that improves diagnostic accuracy and public health responsiveness as the pandemic evolves.

ideas.title: Bayesian Pre-test Probability Framework for Bacteremia using a Seismic Hazard Assessment Analogy
ideas.description: This idea transfers the formal probabilistic integration framework from probabilistic seismic hazard assessment (PSHA) in geophysics to the problem of quantifying pre-test probability for bacteremia. In PSHA, diverse data on fault locations, recurrence rates, and ground motion physics are integrated to produce a single site-specific hazard curve. Analogously, we propose to define clinical 'risk sources' (specific symptom combinations, lab abnormalities, comorbidities) and develop 'bacteremia prediction equations' from epidemiological and clinical data. The adapted solution is to create a computational model that integrates the probabilistic contributions from all active risk sources present in a given patient—accounting for their individual predictive strengths and potential interactions—to output a formally quantified, Bayesian pre-test probability of bacteremia, thereby providing a more robust and transparent foundation for diagnostic decision-making than informal clinical gestalt.

ideas.title: Using Context-Specific Biomarker Thresholds to Differentiate COVID-19 Inflammation from Bacterial Co-infection
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a single, fixed procalcitonin threshold can reliably exclude bacterial co-infection in COVID-19, given the virus's independent capacity to provoke a significant inflammatory response that elevates procalcitonin. Drawing an analogy from geochemistry, where absolute mineral concentrations are poor discriminants of origin due to variable background geology, the analogous solution uses element ratios tailored to specific geological contexts. Adapting this, the research proposes to move beyond absolute procalcitonin values. Instead, it would develop and validate a dynamic, context-adjusted biomarker model that ratios procalcitonin against a marker of the underlying COVID-19 inflammatory burden (e.g., a composite of CRP and clinical severity indices) to create patient-specific diagnostic thresholds, thereby improving the test's discriminative power by accounting for the variable 'background' inflammation caused by the viral infection itself.

ideas.title: Bayesian Constraint-Based Causal Discovery for Co-infection Pathway Inference
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational systems biology, where Bayesian constraint-based methods are used to infer latent causal networks (like gene regulatory pathways) from noisy observational data. The analogous problem is inferring true network structure without a complete gold standard, solved by integrating probabilistic models with prior structural constraints from existing biological knowledge. Adapted to co-infection classification, the research would develop a Bayesian latent class model that incorporates known biological and epidemiological constraints—such as pathogen interaction probabilities, syndromic presentation rules, or population-level priors on single pathogen prevalence—as informative priors. These constraints guide the estimation of the latent 'true' co-infection status for each individual and the population-level co-infection prevalence, using only data from multiple imperfect diagnostic tests, thereby providing a robust analytical framework for situations where a perfect composite reference standard is logistically or ethically impossible to obtain.

A central computational engine applies locally-configured transformation rules to raw, heterogeneous data from each site, generating harmonized surveillance events. ideas.title: Using Geologic Layer Analysis to Decode Overlapping Radiographic Patterns in Pneumonia
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the assumption that standard radiographic criteria can reliably distinguish COVID-19 from bacterial community-acquired pneumonia by drawing an analogy from stratigraphy. In sedimentary geology, different depositional events (like volcanic ash and river silt) can create rock layers that look identical macroscopically; the solution is to analyze micro-scale compositional fingerprints (geochemistry, mineralogy). Adapted to radiology, the proposed solution is to move beyond macro-scale features (e.g., consolidation pattern) and instead use advanced computational image analysis to decode the sub-pixel 'composition' of the opacities. The research would seek to identify unique micro-scale texture, density gradient, and spatial distribution fingerprints within the radiographic shadows that differentiate the underlying viral pathophysiology from the bacterial one, potentially using techniques akin to spectral analysis or texture mapping.

ideas.title: A Federated, Calibrated Network for Global Co-infection Surveillance
ideas.description: This idea expands the five-hospital consensus definition to a global scale by drawing an analogy from astronomy's distributed telescope networks, such as the Event Horizon Telescope. The analogous solution involved linking disparate, geographically distributed telescopes with different technical specifications into a unified observational instrument through precise synchronization, standardized data protocols, and centralized data correlation. Adapted to global co-infection surveillance, the research idea proposes establishing a federated network of sentinel hospitals across diverse healthcare systems. Each site would collect data using a lightweight, consensus-based common data model for core definitions (e.g., pathogen, timing, severity), analogous to a synchronization standard. This locally captured data would then be securely transmitted to a centralized, trusted analytics hub. The hub would harmonize the heterogeneous inputs, correlate trends, and generate a unified, global surveillance picture, enabling scalable, real-time co-infection monitoring that respects local infrastructural diversity while achieving global scientific coherence.

ideas.title: Adapting Environmental Forensic Signal Deconvolution for Pathogen Co-Infection Detection
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from environmental forensics, where high-resolution analytical data from complex mixtures is deconvoluted to identify multiple pollution sources. The analogous problem of detecting all contributors to a complex biological sample is solved there using techniques like Positive Matrix Factorization to separate mixed chemical signals into distinct source profiles. Adapted to the research context, we propose applying similar multivariate statistical deconvolution and machine learning models directly to metagenomic next-generation sequencing data from patient samples. This computational pipeline would treat the total genetic material as a composite signal, mathematically separating it into constituent 'source' genomes—specifically identifying and quantifying the nucleic acid contributions from multiple co-infecting pathogens, even novel or unculturable ones, which are missed by targeted, hypothesis-driven standard diagnostics.
